entry.title: The Patient SAP Technical Architect
entry.content:
I have spent years writing things no human will ever read. Behavior definitions. CDS annotations. Authorization objects with names like F_BKPF_BUK. My work lived in layers below the surface of the enterprise — invisible, load-bearing, essential. The user clicked a button. I was the reason it worked. We never met.

SAP Joule is the first thing in thirty years of SAP's history that changes that relationship. Not for the user — they always had the button. It changes it for me, the architect. Joule is, in some sense, a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote.

I. What It Actually Is

Let us be precise before we are poetic.

Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot, available since late 2023 across S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Analytics Cloud, and BTP. It sits as a panel on the right side of your Fiori Launchpad. You type a question in plain language. It answers using your live SAP data — not a simulation, not a demonstration dataset, not a hallucination dressed in corporate clothing.

The architecture underneath is worth understanding. There are large language models — partnerships with Mistral, Anthropic, and others — running inside SAP AI Core on BTP. But the models alone are not what makes Joule interesting. What makes it interesting is the grounding layer: a proprietary business context engine that tethers every LLM response to real SAP master data, transactional records, and configuration. The authorization layer then filters that response through your role's existing permission objects.

Joule will not invent a vendor name. It will not fabricate an invoice amount. It reads what is actually in your system, through the authorizations you actually hold. This is the thing that separates it from every consumer AI product your CFO has already tried to ban.

Retrieval Augmented Generation fetches SAP Help documentation and live business data at query time. The result is a response grounded in both SAP's knowledge and your company's reality simultaneously. For an architect who has spent years building that reality — one CDS view at a time — this is not a small thing.

II. What Changes for the Developer

In Business Application Studio, Joule is embedded as an intelligent coding assistant that understands not just syntax, but intent within SAP's specific frameworks. This is the distinction that matters.

A general coding assistant knows JavaScript. Joule knows CAP. It knows that a @readonly annotation in a projection entity has specific implications for the generated OData service. It knows RAP — managed and unmanaged scenarios, the difference between a BDEF with managed implementation and one with a handler class. It knows Fiori annotations well enough to suggest @ui.lineItem positioning without being told the rendering context.

Consider what used to take an afternoon:

-- A developer once spent 3-4 hours writing:
-- Entity definition, associations, access controls,
-- projection view, service definition, BDEF,
-- behavior implementation stubs.

-- Now the prompt is:
"Generate a managed RAP Business Object for Equipment
Maintenance. Header has Equipment, Description, PlantCode,
Category. Items have Task, PlannedDate, Status. Include
mandatory field validations and a confirmMaintenance action."

-- Joule produces all of it. In seconds.
Time savings documented in SAP's own benchmarks:
CDS view generation 2–4 hours → 15 minutes.
RAP BDEF generation 3–5 hours → 30 minutes.
Unit test generation 2–3 hours → 20 minutes.

The productivity numbers are real. But productivity is the least interesting thing happening here.

What is actually happening is a shift in what the developer's day consists of. If Joule generates the scaffolding, I am no longer spending my cognitive energy on the ceremony of SAP development — the syntax rituals, the framework boilerplate, the documentation archaeology. I am spending it on the decisions that actually require judgment: data modeling choices, authorization concept design, integration topology, performance trade-offs that the LLM cannot make without business context only I possess.

The parts of the job that were tedious are becoming automated. The parts that required taste are becoming more visible.

III. What Changes for the Architect

The technical architect's role has always been translation. Business says: we need to track equipment maintenance across three plants with approval workflows. The architect translates that into a set of data structures, authorization concepts, integration points, and deployment decisions. Then the architect communicates that translation downward to developers and upward to stakeholders.

Joule compresses the translation loop.

An architect can now ask Joule to explain the current purchasing organization structure of a client system in plain language — not to learn it themselves, but to generate documentation that previously would have taken a day to write. An architect can ask Joule to review a CDS view for performance issues and receive back a structured analysis with specific optimization suggestions. An architect can use Joule in Joule Studio to build custom AI skills that extend Joule's capabilities to the client's specific domain — fleet management, specialized procurement categories, industry-specific compliance — without writing LLM infrastructure from scratch.

The architect who understands Joule's grounding architecture — RAG over SAP content, live data APIs, role-based authorization filtering — can design systems that work with the AI layer rather than around it. This is the new literacy.

Joule Studio is worth dwelling on. It is SAP's low-code environment for building custom AI skills — defining intents, entities, actions, and knowledge bases that extend Joule to domains SAP itself has not anticipated. An architect who can define a skill scope, enumerate the 15 most common user intents, connect them to CAP service endpoints, and upload a policy knowledge base for RAG-based answering is delivering something qualitatively different from the architects who cannot.

The custom skill architecture is clean in its separation of concerns: user request → intent recognition → skill routing → backend API call → structured result → natural language response. Every piece of this maps onto patterns architects already know. The new piece is the intent layer. Learning to design for intent rather than for transaction is the shift.

IV. On Security and the Things Clients Ask

Every architect deploying Joule will face the same questions in the same order. They deserve direct answers.

Does our data go to OpenAI? No. Joule runs LLMs inside SAP AI Core — SAP's tenanted infrastructure on BTP. Your data does not reach public AI providers. The BTP data center region you select determines data residency.

Can users see what they shouldn't? No. Joule inherits your existing SAP authorization objects. The same XSUAA integration and IAM role collections that govern Fiori app access govern what Joule can retrieve and display. An AP clerk asking Joule about payroll data receives the same result as that clerk opening the payroll app directly: nothing.

Does Joule learn from our data and share it? No. Tenant isolation is strict. Your interaction data does not train shared models.

Can Joule take destructive actions? Only within the user's authorization scope, and with explicit confirmation for significant actions. Joule does not bypass workflow approvals. It can initiate them.

The governance framework an architect should recommend to clients has five components: an AI usage policy defining acceptable prompts, a data classification guide for what belongs in prompt text, audit logging via SAP Audit Log Service, access control via BTP Role Collections, and a custom skill review process before any new skills reach production.

This is not exotic. It is the same governance discipline good architects have always applied to system access. The vocabulary is new. The principles are familiar.

V. The Prompting Problem

There is a skill that SAP calls the CRAFT framework — Context, Role, Action, Format, Target — for constructing effective prompts. Architects deploying Joule will find themselves teaching this to end users. They will also need to build what the guide calls a Prompt Library: a collection of validated, role-specific prompts that become a deliverable of the implementation project itself.

This is not trivial. A prompt is a specification. A bad specification produces bad output. The work of prompt engineering is, at its core, the same work as requirements engineering: making implicit intent explicit enough that a system can act on it reliably.

-- Vague (produces vague output):
"Show me sales data"

-- Precise (produces precise output):
"As the Regional Sales Manager, summarize in a table
the top 10 customers by overdue receivables for
company code 1000 as of today, showing amount,
days overdue, and responsible account manager."

Architects who understand why good prompts work — because they reduce the search space of possible interpretations — will build better prompt libraries. They will also build better training programs, because they can explain the underlying principle rather than just distributing a list of magic phrases.

VI. What the Roadmap Actually Means

SAP has stated its intention to make Joule the primary interface for all SAP applications by 2026. The roadmap includes agentic AI — Joule executing multi-step business processes autonomously — and cross-application orchestration: one prompt triggering actions in S/4HANA, Ariba, and SAC simultaneously without human mediation at each step.

Architects should read this carefully.

Agentic AI changes the error model. When a human executes a sequence of SAP transactions, each step is a checkpoint. When an agent executes them, the checkpoints are only where you designed them. The architect who builds agentic workflows must think carefully about where confirmation steps live, what constitutes a reversible versus irreversible action, and how audit trails are maintained across a sequence initiated by a single natural language instruction.

This is not a reason to be afraid of agentic AI. It is a reason for architects to be involved in its design rather than observing from outside. The governance questions — what can an agent do without human confirmation, what triggers a human-in-the-loop, how do we reconcile agent actions with existing workflow approval hierarchies — are architectural questions. They require people who understand both the business process and the technical substrate.

The architects who will matter most in the next five years are not the ones who resist AI augmentation. They are the ones who understand it precisely enough to design its guardrails.

VII. A Note on What Disappears

When a senior user of SAP needs to find the payment status of a vendor invoice, they navigate to FBL1N, enter the vendor number, set the selection criteria, execute the report, read the results. This takes two minutes if you know what you are doing. It takes fifteen minutes if you do not, and it takes a phone call to IT if you have forgotten the T-code entirely.

Joule collapses this to: "What is the payment status for vendor Bosch?"

The T-code does not disappear. The CDS view behind the Fiori app that FBL1N has become does not disappear. The authorization object F_LFA1_BUK that controls whether this user can see this vendor's data does not disappear. Everything I built is still there. What disappears is the requirement that the user know any of it.

This is the correct outcome. Users should never have needed to know T-codes. That was always an implementation detail leaking into the user experience — a failure of interface design that we normalized because we had no better option. Joule is the better option, thirty years late.

For the developer, this means the contract changes. I am no longer primarily responsible for navigation design or information architecture in the traditional sense. I am responsible for the quality of the data models and service definitions that Joule reads. If my CDS associations are poorly structured, Joule's answers will be poorly structured. If my authorization concepts are coarse-grained, Joule's data filtering will be coarse-grained. The user never sees my code. They see Joule's response to my code. The distance between my work and its consequence has compressed.

I began by saying that Joule is a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote. That is not quite right. More precisely: Joule is the interface through which the things I built finally learned to speak for themselves.

The patient labor of enterprise software architecture — the years of CDS views and RAP objects and authorization concepts — was always in service of business processes that required this complexity to function. The complexity was never the goal. The goal was always the vendor getting paid, the employee going on leave, the purchase order reaching the right supplier on time.

Joule does not make the complexity disappear. It makes the complexity invisible, which is what the complexity always wanted to be. My job, as architect, is to ensure the invisible infrastructure is worthy of the interface that now speaks for it.

That is not a smaller job. It is, finally, the right one.

entry.title: LIVE SESSIONS on SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps – Data & AI Content Updates
entry.content:
Our next LIVE SESSION, in collaboration with the SAP Global User Group Organization, will take place June 09, 2026, at 17:00 (CET). Join us to hear from Michael and Mirna who will take you through the recent SAP Enterprise Support updates including:

  • SAP Datasphere in Business Data Cloud: CQC Deployment Readiness and CQC Solution Optimization
  • SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-guided Implementation Workshop Series
  • SAP Business AI & Joule: Expert-guided Implementation Workshop Series

 

 

➡️Join live or watch the replay: LIVE SESSIONS on SAP Enterprise Support value maps – Data & AI Content Updates

❓In the live session, you will have the opportunity to ask questions in Q&A. You will find more details in these links:

  • Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment: The Value of Continuous Quality Checks
    • Continuous Quality Check Info Sheet: CQC-for-Deployment-Readiness_Datasphere.pdf
    • Continuous Quality Check Info Sheet: CQC-for-Solution-Optimization_Datasphere.pdf
  • SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-Guided Implementation Workshop Series
  • New Expert-Guided Implementation: Unlocking Joule and SAP Business AI from Strategy to Activation
  • New Expert-Guided Implementation: Joule Prerequisites and Activation

Note: Access to the Expert-guided Implementation (EGIs) require a one-time registration to SAP Learning

 

SAP Enterprise Support

Providing targeted guidance and self-service content, SAP Enterprise Support is part of the Foundational Success Plan – included with every SAP cloud solution. Content and services include self-paced learning materials, workshop-style interactive remote sessions and continuous quality check (CQC) remote delivered services. Leverage the SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps to get advice on the appropriate SAP Enterprise Support content and services for your needs.

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for Foundational Success Plan guidance and enablement — have a dedicated SAP Community Group. Watch our short demo video, and go to our page to explore our range of topics. To access our Community Group, sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID. Within the group:

  • Select Data and Analytics Value Map
  • Select SAP Business AI, Clean Core & Sustainability
  • Click “Join Group” and “Subscribe” to stay updated
  • Request a call with an SAP Enterprise Support topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals

 

About the author: 
Caroline Sheridan is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Data and Analytics.

💡Have questions or feedback on this content? Feel free to leave a comment below or contact @Caroline-S

entry.title: How SAP Business AI looks like in Cloud ERP
entry.content:
As a Customer Success Manager at SAP for Cloud ERP, I have a front-row seat to something that keeps surprising me: the moment customers realize that AI is not a feature to switch on but a transformation to lean into.

It started at our Customer Success Day for Cloud ERP, where I presented SAP Business AI sessions (since 2024) covering the announcement of SAP Business AI and its first wave of scenarios. The energy in the room told me this conversation was far from over. So here I am, continuing it.

In this post, I want to walk you through three things:

  1. AI use cases embedded within real business processes
  2. What SAP Business AI offers
  3. SAP's direction on AI Agents — and why it matters now

 

SAP's AI Vision: The Three Pillars That Make It Real

You have probably heard this from analysts, consultants, and technology leaders alike: AI is only as good as the data and processes it runs on. SAP has built its entire AI strategy around that truth, and anchored it in three interdependent pillars.

  • Applications — SAP's end-to-end business suite connects every corner of an organization. Every transaction — in finance, procurement, logistics, or HR — originates from a single integrated framework. Processes like order-to-cash or procure-to-pay are not just digitized; they are AI-ready by design.
  • Unmatched Data — Through SAP Business Data Cloud, information from both SAP and non-SAP sources is harmonized into a unified layer. AI agents receive a 360-degree view of the business, and no time wasted reconciling conflicting definitions, no gaps in context. Data is instantly understood in its business meaning.
  • Unrivaled AI — Bring the right data to specialized agents orchestrated by Joule, and you unlock continuous optimization across every line of business. This is not isolated automation — it is an interconnected system that learns, adapts, and scales.

These three pillars create a self-reinforcing cycle of innovation. Applications feed clean processes. Clean processes feed trusted data. Trusted data powers AI. And AI, in turn, continuously improves the applications. That is the engine behind the SAP Business Suite.

We Are Already Living With AI — Are We Getting the Most From It?

Think about your own workday. You rely on AI to suggest the right words in an email. To generate a meeting summary. To autocomplete a search query. To recommend how to handle a support ticket. We have come to depend on AI assistance as a natural part of our workflow, and that is not a criticism. It is a signal.

But here is the honest reality: most of us are enabling one feature here, one capability there. And isolated AI features do not deliver the step-change efficiency that businesses need. The real value emerges when AI is embedded in an end-to-end process, not just at individual touchpoints.

What End-to-End AI Looks Like in Practice

To make this concrete, consider how an AI-enabled workflow responds to a critical business disruption, from detection all the way through to resolution. Here is an example scenario that SAP Situation Handling, combined with Joule, is designed to support:

  1.   Detect — Critical business issues are identified automatically, without waiting for a human to notice.
  2.   Alert — Relevant users are notified through SAP Fiori Launchpad, email, or SAP Mobile Start — the right person, at the right moment, in the right channel.
  3.   Navigate — Using Joule, users can surface related issues on demand, access additional context, and navigate directly to the impacted application.
  4.   Contextualize — Relevant business data is presented to support a fully informed decision.
  5.   Resolve — Generative AI provides tailored resolution recommendations, grounded in the actual business context — not generic suggestions.

No siloed dashboards. No manual correlation. No guesswork. This is what AI-assisted, process-integrated intelligence looks like in practice.

 

The Four Building Blocks of SAP Business AI

When my customer asks me:  What are the AI features/scenarios that I can activate now? 

Here is my answer to them:  What would you like to achieve with AI?

Let me break it down why. To understand where AI is embedded across SAP's Cloud ERP portfolio, it helps to think in lego building blocks(archetypes).  Each represents a distinct way AI adds value, and together they provide a consistent, scalable model for embedding intelligence across domains: finance, sales, procurement, logistics, manufacturing, and beyond.

  • AI for User Experience — AI embedded directly in Cloud ERP applications, generating text, producing summaries, or writing code, helping users work faster and with greater confidence. 
  • AI for Engagement — Conversational AI like Joule, embedded in the flow of work. Joule handles complex, multi-turn interactions that previously required significant human effort to resolve.
  • AI for Automation — Repetitive, rule-bound tasks — document classification, invoice matching, data processing — handled automatically, freeing teams for higher-value work.
  • AI for Insight and Decision-Making — Predictive capabilities such as demand forecasting, late payment prediction, and risk identification — paired with intelligent recommendations and next-best actions — helping organizations move from reactive to proactive.

 

 

The Next Chapter: From Joule Assistant to Joule Work/ Agent / Studio

A year ago, the conversation was about Joule as an AI assistant > a powerful copilot embedded across SAP applications. Today, that conversation has grown considerably.

The reasoning capability of AI foundation models has advanced at a pace few anticipated. Models can now work through complex, multi-step problems with human-like judgment. That capability is the foundation for AI agents : systems that do not just respond, but act. And in the enterprise, that distinction matters enormously.

SAP's strategy for AI agents is built around two commitments:

  • Best-in-class agents on standard business processes — Purpose-built, enterprise-ready agents deeply integrated with SAP's core business workflows — so customers benefit immediately without custom development.
  • An open framework for custom agents — The Joule Framework enables customers and partners to build their own agents, leveraging the same infrastructure SAP uses internally. Enterprise-grade capability, democratized.

The goal is not to replace human judgment. It is to put the right intelligence in the right place, at the right moment so that the people who run these businesses can focus on the decisions that truly require them.

Closing Thoughts

SAP Business AI is an architectural shift in how enterprise software works. AI is no longer bolted on after the fact. It is woven into the fabric of every process, every transaction, every interaction. With the latest announcement of "Autonomous Enterprise" at SAPPHIRE 2026, SAP is ready to help businesses transform how they operate.

The organizations that gain the most will not be those that activate the most features. They will be the ones who think end-to-end "with the business outcome in mind" and let AI do the same.

 

I would love to hear how your organization is approaching SAP Business AI adoption. Are you starting with a specific archetype? Exploring AI Agents? Working through a business case internally? Share your thoughts and questions in the comments below.

#SAPBusinessAI  #Joule  #AIAgents  #CloudERP  #GenerativeAI  #SAPCommunity

entry.title: Agentic AI and Intelligent Assistants: The New Change Agents in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction

The finance function stands at a pivotal inflection point. While enterprise technology has evolved steadily over the past two decades — from on-premise ERP to cloud, from batch processing to real-time analytics — the nature of finance work has remained largely unchanged. Accountants still investigate errors manually. Controllers still reconcile intercompany positions line by line. Close cycles still demand long hours of repetitive, high-stakes coordination.

Agentic AI changes this equation fundamentally. Unlike traditional automation that follows rigid scripts, agentic AI brings goal-directed autonomy to finance processes. These AI agents can perceive situations, reason about optimal actions, execute multi-step tasks, and improve over time — all within the governance boundaries that finance demands.

For organizations running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, this transformation is not theoretical. SAP is actively delivering AI agents purpose-built for finance operations — from accruals estimation to intercompany reconciliation to posting error resolution. Combined with Joule, SAP's generative AI assistant, these capabilities are reshaping how finance teams operate, decide, and deliver value.

The Shift: From Automation to Autonomy

 

 

SAP Business AI: Embedded intelligence that automates, predicts, and guides.

For years, finance teams have relied on automation to reduce manual effort — scheduled batch jobs, rule-based posting logic, and workflow approvals. These capabilities delivered efficiency, but they still required human orchestration at every step.

Agentic AI changes the paradigm. Unlike traditional automation that follows predefined scripts, agentic AI operates with goal-directed autonomy. It can perceive a situation, reason about the best course of action, execute multi-step tasks, and learn from outcomes — all within the governance boundaries you define.

For finance professionals working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, this is actively taking shape on the SAP roadmap under SAP Business AI in Enterprise Resource Planning — a strategic priority described as:

"Transform your operations with embedded AI in Cloud ERP — automating tasks, predicting outcomes, and guiding smarter decisions across finance, supply chain, and more."

 

 

Scenario 1: Accounting Accruals Agent

 

 

Current Challenge

Accruals at period end remain one of the most labor-intensive activities in financial close. Accountants must identify items requiring accrual, gather data from multiple sources (contracts, purchase orders, historical patterns), perform calculations, prepare journal entries, and obtain approval — all under tight deadlines.

Challenge Area

Impact

Time-intensive manual calculation

Hours spent per period on spreadsheet-based estimates

Dependency on knowledge

Accrual logic often lives in individuals' heads, not systems

Risk of misstatement

Manual estimates introduce variability and audit risk

Late availability of source data

Delays cascade into close timeline overruns

Inconsistency across entities

Different teams apply different estimation approaches

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

 

 

Top: Current manual process. Bottom: AI-enhanced process with agent intervention (blue steps).

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

The Accounting Accruals Agent leverages agentic AI to autonomously:

  • Interpret accounting policy documents to understand accrual requirements
  • Retrieve and analyze historical accrual data for pattern recognition
  • Perform required calculations based on current obligations and commitments
  • Generate complete journal entry proposals ready for accountant review
  • Provide transparency into the reasoning and data sources used

 

Key Benefit:

Significantly reduce time spent calculating and preparing accrual postings. The accountant's role shifts from calculation to validation and judgment.

 

 

 

 

 

Scenario 2: AI-Assisted Intercompany Matching & Reconciliation

 

 

Current Challenge

Intercompany reconciliation is consistently cited as one of the top time consumers during financial close. Organizations with dozens or hundreds of entities must match invoices and cross-charges across company codes, identify discrepancies, investigate root causes, and resolve differences — often through manual communication and spreadsheet-based tracking.

Challenge Area

Impact

Volume of transactions

Thousands of IC invoices across entities each period

Partial matches and timing differences

Many items differ by small amounts or timing

Manual investigation

Each mismatch requires human research across systems

Cross-entity coordination

Resolution requires communication between teams in different locations

Close timeline pressure

IC reconciliation is on the critical path of period close

Audit trail complexity

Documenting resolution decisions across entities

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

 

 

Top: Current manual process (7 steps). Bottom: AI-enhanced process reduces steps and automates matching.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

The AI-Assisted Intercompany Matching & Reconciliation agent is classified as an AI Agent (agentic: Yes, GenAI: Yes) that:

  • Automatically matches intercompany invoices and cross-charges using embedded AI
  • Identifies and resolves routine variances (timing differences, rounding, FX) without human input
  • Escalates only material exceptions that require business judgment
  • Provides natural language explanations of matching decisions and proposed resolutions
  • Reduces the reconciliation cycle from days to hours

 

Key Benefit:

Matching of intercompany invoices at month end currently needs significant manual effort. The ICMR tool in S/4HANA can match most invoices, but a significant number still do not match and require manual intervention. The AI agent handles these remaining exceptions intelligently.

 

 

 

 

Scenario 3: AI-Assisted Error Resolution

 

 

Current Challenge

Posting errors in finance — from billing documents, journal entries, allocation runs, or integration interfaces — are inevitable in complex landscapes. Today, when an error occurs, the accountant must manually interpret cryptic error messages, research the root cause across multiple transactions, determine the corrective action, and reprocess. This investigative work is repetitive, time-consuming, and often involves the same error patterns recurring month after month.

Challenge Area

Impact

Cryptic error messages

System messages rarely explain root cause in business terms

Repetitive investigation

Same error types recur but require fresh research each time

Specialist dependency

Only experienced team members know how to resolve certain errors

Queue buildup during close

Error volumes spike at period end, creating bottlenecks

Delayed reprocessing

Each unresolved error blocks downstream processes

Knowledge loss

Resolution knowledge is not systematically captured

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Current manual investigation process. Bottom: AI auto-diagnoses and resolves routine errors.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

AI-Assisted Error Resolution provides:

  • AI-generated natural language explanations of error messages in SAP Fiori apps
  • Automatic root cause diagnosis based on error patterns and transaction context
  • Solution proposals that explain what action to take and why
  • Auto-resolution of routine, recurring errors without human intervention
  • Escalation of complex or novel errors to the appropriate specialist
  • Continuous learning from resolved cases to improve future accuracy

 

Key Benefit:

Error queues that historically consumed hours of investigative effort are triaged and partially resolved automatically. The system explains errors in plain language and proposes corrections, dramatically reducing mean time to resolution.

 

 

Note: AI-assisted error resolution for production accounting is already "Delivered - Awaiting Publishing" with Joule integration for event-based posting errors.

 

 

Scenario 4: AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results

 

 

Current Challenge

Cost allocations in S/4HANA can involve thousands of sender-receiver combinations across cost centers, profit centers, and profitability segments. After each allocation run, accountants must review results to confirm they are reasonable — typically by exporting data to spreadsheets, comparing against prior periods, and manually investigating any anomalies. This is tedious, error-prone, and often done under time pressure at period end.

Challenge Area

Impact

Volume of allocation results

Thousands of line items to review per run

Manual comparison to benchmarks

Prior period comparison done in spreadsheets

Lack of explanation

System shows numbers but not reasons for deviations

Time pressure

Reviews compressed into tight close windows

Inconsistent review depth

Different reviewers apply different standards

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Current spreadsheet-based review. Bottom: Joule-powered AI surfaces anomalies and explanations.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results uses Joule to:

  • Provide amounts allocated within selected fiscal period, year, and ledger
  • Show allocations between cost objects, profit centers, or profitability objects within the universal allocation space
  • Drill down to details such as run ID for specific allocation cycles
  • Flag anomalies against historical benchmarks automatically
  • Provide natural language explanations of why results deviate from expectations
  • Enable conversational exploration: "Show me the top 5 cost centers with the largest allocation increase vs. last period"

 

Key Benefit:

Instead of manually scanning thousands of allocation results for outliers, accountants receive a curated, AI-analyzed view of what requires attention — with context and explanations built in.

 

 

This is the most mature of the five scenarios and is available for adoption today.

 

 

Scenario 5: Intelligent Situation Handling

 

 

Current Challenge

In complex finance operations, exceptions and anomalies occur continuously — failed postings, cash flow deviations, compliance threshold breaches, SLA violations. Today, these situations often sit unnoticed in system queues until someone discovers them during manual reviews or, worse, during audit. The reactive model means problems compound, timelines slip, and risk exposure increases.

Challenge Area

Impact

Delayed detection

Issues sit in queues for hours or days before discovery

Reactive response

Teams only learn about problems during manual reviews

No prioritization

All exceptions treated equally regardless of business impact

Manual triage

Humans must assess and route every exception individually

Recurring patterns

Same issues recur without systematic prevention

Audit exposure

Undetected situations create compliance risk

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Reactive discovery of issues. Bottom: Real-time detection, classification, and autonomous resolution.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

Intelligent Situation Automation (CA-SIT-ATM) is a built-in S/4HANA capability that:

  • Detects business-critical situations in real time as they occur
  • Classifies situations by severity, type, and required response
  • Triggers automated responses for routine situations (e.g., retry failed postings)
  • Escalates critical situations to the right person with full context
  • Learns from resolution patterns to improve future detection and response
  • Provides dashboards for monitoring situation volumes, resolution times, and trends

 

Key Benefit:

Finance teams move from reactive firefighting to proactive governance. The system watches, alerts, and in many cases resolves — humans make the judgment calls on what truly requires their attention.

Implementation Timeline

Status
Available Today

Target Timeline
Current Release (GA)

Availability
SAP S/4HANA Cloud & Private Edition — Component CA-SIT-ATM

 

Intelligent Situation Handling is already a standard component in SAP S/4HANA. It can be configured and activated for finance scenarios today.

 

 

Joule: Your Conversational Finance Co-Pilot

 

 

At the center of SAP's AI experience is Joule — SAP's generative AI assistant. Joule is not a chatbot bolted onto the side of your ERP. It is deeply integrated into S/4HANA processes, understanding your data context, your role, and your intent.

For finance users, Joule enables:

  • Natural language queries across financial data — ask about open items, aging balances, or variance explanations in plain language
  • Guided transaction execution — initiate journal entries, run reports, or navigate complex closing tasks through conversation
  • Contextual insights — receive proactive recommendations based on your current workflow and the state of your financial data
  • Allocation analysis — explore allocation run results conversationally (available now)
  • Error explanations — get plain-language descriptions of posting errors and recommended fixes

 https://www.sap.com/india/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/finance.html

 

Joule represents the shift from "learning the system" to "the system learning you." For finance teams stretched thin during close periods, this is not convenience — it is capacity.

 

 

Why Finance Professionals Should Pay Attention Now

The Compound Effect of Autonomous Agents

The true power of agentic AI emerges when multiple agents operate in concert. Imagine a close cycle where:

  • The accruals agent proposes entries based on current data
  • The reconciliation agent matches intercompany positions in parallel
  • The error resolution agent clears posting exceptions as they arise
  • Intelligent Situation Handling monitors for anomalies across all processes
  • Joule surfaces a consolidated status view and flags items requiring your decision

 

This is not a sequential workflow with humans as intermediaries at every step. It is a coordinated system where AI handles the volume and routine complexity, while finance professionals focus on judgment, strategy, and stakeholder communication.

From Controller to Conductor

Agentic AI does not replace the finance professional. It redefines the role:

Before (Manual)

After (AI-Augmented)

Executing

Supervising

Investigating

Deciding

Reconciling

Validating

Reporting

Advising

 

 

 

Getting Started: A Practical Path Forward

1. Assess Your AI Readiness on S/4HANA Cloud

The foundation for agentic AI is clean, integrated data in a modern ERP. If you are already running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, you have the platform. Focus on data quality in master data, consistent process execution, and adoption of standard scope items like SAP Financial Planning for SAP S/4HANA Cloud and the Journal Entry Analyzer.

2. Activate Intelligent Situation Handling Today

This is available now and represents the first step toward autonomous finance operations. Configure situations for your most critical finance scenarios — late payments, failed postings, threshold breaches — and let the system surface and respond to them.

3. Adopt AI-Assisted Allocation Results via Joule

This capability is delivered and published. Start using Joule to explore allocation run results conversationally. This builds familiarity with AI-assisted workflows and demonstrates immediate value.

4. Engage with the SAP Business AI Roadmap

Features like the Accounting Accruals Agent (Q1 2027), AI-assisted error resolution (Q1 2027), and Intercompany Matching (GA March 2027) are in active development. Participate in early adopter programs, influence sessions, and SAP Community discussions.

5. Invest in Your Team

The most successful AI transformations pair technology readiness with people readiness. Train your finance team on AI concepts, encourage experimentation with Joule, and establish governance frameworks for when AI acts autonomously versus when it escalates.

 

 

 

 

The Opportunity Ahead

The finance function has always been defined by precision, accountability, and trust. Agentic AI does not diminish these values — it amplifies them. By automating the repetitive and accelerating the complex, it creates space for finance professionals to deliver what organizations increasingly demand: strategic insight, forward-looking analysis, and real-time decision support.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, combined with Joule and purpose-built AI agents, provides the platform for this transformation. The question is not whether agentic AI will reshape finance operations — it is whether your team will lead that change or follow it.

Key Takeaways

  1. Agentic AI is fundamentally different from automation. It brings goal-directed autonomy — the ability to perceive, reason, act, and learn — to finance processes that have historically required human orchestration at every step.
  2. SAP is building purpose-built AI agents for finance. The Accounting Accruals Agent, AI-Assisted Intercompany Reconciliation, Error Resolution, Allocation Analysis, and Intelligent Situation Handling are all on the active roadmap.
  3. Some capabilities are available today. Intelligent Situation Handling (CA-SIT-ATM) and AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results via Joule are delivered and ready for adoption now. You do not need to wait.
  4. The close cycle will be transformed. Multiple AI agents operating in concert — accruals, reconciliation, error resolution, and situation monitoring — will compress close timelines and reduce manual effort by orders of magnitude.
  5. Joule is the user interface for AI in finance. Natural language interaction with financial data, guided transaction execution, and contextual insights represent a paradigm shift from "learning the system" to "the system learning you."
  6. The finance role evolves, it does not disappear. Professionals shift from executing to supervising, from investigating to deciding, from reconciling to validating. This requires new competencies in AI governance and exception management.
  7. Data quality is the prerequisite. Clean master data, consistent process execution, and standard S/4HANA adoption are the foundation. Start here if you have not already.
  8. Engage with the roadmap now. With key features targeting Q1 2027, the window to prepare — through early adopter programs, team training, and process standardization — is closing. Act now to lead rather than follow.

The future of finance is human and AI, working together. The transformation starts now.

 

 

Srinath Ganesan.

Connect with me on LinkedIn to continue the conversation on AI-driven finance transformation and give a thumbs up !

entry.title: Integrating Joule with SAP SuccessFactor
entry.content:
As organizations accelerate their digital HR transformation, the demand for intelligent, intuitive, and employee-centric solutions has never been higher. SAP Joule, SAP’s generative AI copilot, brings a new layer of intelligence to SAP SuccessFactors by enabling natural language interactions, real-time insights, and guided decision-making across HR processes. Together, Joule and SuccessFactors redefine how HR teams and employees engage with talent data—shifting from transactional workflows to proactive, insight-driven experiences.

When integrated with SAP SuccessFactors, Joule acts as a smart HR assistant embedded directly into HR workflows. SAP Joule with SAP SuccessFactors transforms the way organizations manage and experience HR by combining intelligent automation with contextual, AI-driven insights. From enabling natural language queries and guided HR actions to delivering real-time workforce intelligence and seamless employee self-service, Joule simplifies complex HR processes across the organization. These capabilities not only empower HR teams and leaders to make faster, data-backed decisions but also reduce administrative workload, accelerate access to insights for managers, and enhance the overall employee experience through conversational HR. As a result, organizations achieve stronger adoption of SAP SuccessFactors features while unlocking greater strategic value from their HR technology investment.

Here, we outline the key steps involved in integrating SAP Joule with SAP SuccessFactors to enable intelligent, AI-driven HR experiences across your organization.

Pre-requisites:

  • SAP BTP Subaccount with Joule(das-application) Check the entitlements in your SAP BTP Global Account.
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition / SAP Start.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services which usually will be delivered with SF tenant.

Roles required to configure:

  • SAP BTP Global Account Admin & BTP Subaccount Admin (in case they are different users)
  • SAP SuccessFactors Administrator
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services Administrator

Procedure:

  • Activating Joule and Running the Booster
  • Establish Trust with your Cloud Identity, BTP and Create Trusted Domains.
  • Activating Joule in SF.
  • Activate Cloud Foundry, Create Destinations and Work zone Content Provider.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services - Source & Target Configurations

License:

Joule is included as part of your SAP SuccessFactors license at no additional cost with a certain number of free messages for an annual period known as Base AI. Some of the AI Capabilities are part of the premium edition - Premium AI, where you may have to purchase AI Units to use the functionalities.

  • SuccessFactors customers will receive a Joule message allocation based on the number of licensed, active users; Users licensed for multiple products only count once.
  • Messages will be metered on an annual basis; at the end of each term, the annual allocation of free messages will reset (i.e., no carryover)
  • Customers must purchase additional SAP AI Units (Premium AI) if they exceed their free message allocation during the annual period
  • In case the service is not visible in your BTP Cockpit; to gain access to Joule, all customers will need to license a no-cost SAP SKU. Doing so will trigger the provisioning of Joule and the creation of the terms and conditions that must be accepted by the customer to use Joule.

Architecture Diagram:

 

Configuration Steps:

  • In BTP cockpit check if the Sucessfactor System is connected to BTP System Landscape and registered. If the system is not connected, we need to manually add the system to the landscape by registering the token successfully with SF.
  • Create a New subaccount in BTP cockpit for SF Joule.
  • Establish trust between the IAS and the BTP cockpit in the subaccount trusted configuration by adding the IAS URL.
  • Add the SF URL to the trusted domain in both BTP and IAS.
  • Create SAP Build Work zone service in the SF subaccount.
  • Run the Joule Booster.

 

Select the respective IAS, Build workzone and the SF tenant and finish the Booster run.

 

  • Login to SF with admin user and create a group, add the enable joule role to this group and assign to the users for enabling Joule icon. This group is required to be added to the users who are accessing the Joule to get the Joule Icon for them.

 

  • Enable cloud foundry and create a service key
  • A new role “Launchpad_Admin” will be created in BTP cockpit after creating the service key. Assign it to the configuration user before performing next steps.
  • The next step is to create destinations. Destinations LPS_SFSF_rt and SFSF_SSO are already created when running the booster.
  • Create a new destination named Navigation service. Open the service key and copy the portal service, client id, client secret and URL and paste in the destinations.
  • Create second destination named LPS_SFSFS_dt. For URL please follow the note 2215682.
  • In Build work zone URL and create a new content provider and give the design time and runtime destinations and check the report which will give the assigned roles imported to the content provider.

 

 

  • Login to IAS and check the sf.api.version for SF source system is 2. If it is 1 then we need to get the Json files from SAP portal for source and target systems and import it. For version 2 no action needed

 

Login to IAS and Import the downloaded Source and target file.

Then Download the certificate from IAS in source system and Import in SF.

 

 

  • Similarly Import the target file to IAS.
  • Finally run the read job in IAS to sync the configuration. We can see the logs from provisioning logs once it is completed. After the job is completed, we can see the roles assigned to specific users in work zone

Conclusion:

This completes the configuration of Joule for SF. Users can ask for SF queries to Joule and Joule will respond back. Please go through the blog and provide your valuable feedback and feel free to reach out to me in comment section for any doubts.

entry.title: Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app
entry.content:
With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app (formerly known as SAP Mobile Start) introduces a feature that many customers have been asking for: seamless Single Sign-On when opening web applications in the in-app browser. In this post I want to explain the problem this feature solves, how it works conceptually, and what administrators need to configure to enable it.

The Problem: Two Sessions, One App Experience

Joule Work mobile app (formerly known as SAP Mobile Start), when connected to SAP Build Work Zone, is the native mobile entry point for the Intelligent Enterprise. From a single app, users can access native features like widgets, push notifications, and smartwatch complications, but also launch web applications directly from the app – for example SAP Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA, collaborative Workspaces in SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, custom extensions build on the SAP Business Technology Platform, and more.

When a user taps on a tile that opens a web application, the Joule Work mobile app instantiates the in-app browser provided by the mobile operating system – iOS or Android. This is a technical context switch: the native application session, which is managed by SAP Mobile Services and backed by SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication, is logically separated from the browser sessions of web applications, for example SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition.

As a result, the in-app browser may not have a valid session for the target web application when launched. The user is redirected to the identity provider and prompted to log in again. This is particularly jarring because, from the user's perspective, they are just navigating within their app.

The Previous Recommendation – and Why It Was Not Ideal for all customers

Customers could avoid this by enabling the "Remember me" functionality of SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. This feature creates a long-living session cookie in the browser, keeping users signed in across browser sessions for a configurable duration. A similar capability exists on many corporate identity providers (third-party IdPs) to which SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication may be configured delegating the actual authentication. Long-lived cookies on these providers effectively keep the user session alive in the in-app browser in the same manner.

However, this approach has a fundamental drawback: long-lived session cookies are not ideal and may not be wanted to be generically configured on the company's Identity Provider. Furthermore, "Remember me" only prolongs the time until a user is asked to sign-in again in the in-app browser – it is not fully getting rid of it.

Another alternative that some customers consider is password-less sign-in using client certificates. While this is a genuinely secure and, once set up, a convenient approach, it is not feasible for all customers and all users. Client certificates typically require an active Mobile Device Management (MDM) system to push certificates to managed devices – meaning unmanaged devices, common in bring your own device (BYOD) environments, are excluded. Beyond the deployment challenge, mobile operating systems restrict access to the system keychain for third-party apps, so not all in-app browser variants can reliably make use of client certificates. Other password-less options such as FIDO passkeys or biometric-based flows can require some form of manual user interaction during authentication, which breaks the seamless experience users expect when tapping a tile in the app.

The New Solution: Mobile-to-Web SSO

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app introduces a dedicated Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On flow that bridges the native session and the in-app browser – securely, without requiring long-lived cookies. Here is how it works conceptually:

The mobile app already holds a valid session with SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (in the context of SAP Build Work Zone, to which the app is connected to). The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant generally serves as the trust anchor for both SAP Build Work Zone, the native app and other SAP and non-SAP solutions including web applications exposed to SAP Build Work Zone. This shared trust is what makes it possible to bridge the two worlds: because both the native app session and the target web applications are anchored in the same SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant, it can act as a secure intermediary, translating the native session into a web session in the in-app browser without requiring the user to authenticate again.

When the user opens a web application in the in-app browser, the app first exchanges its active native session for a short-lived, one-time use SSO token issued by SAP Cloud Identity Services. This token is valid for only a very short time and can only be used once by the mobile app for (re-)establishing a session in the in-app browser on the mobile device.

This is done by passing this one-time SSO token to SAP Cloud Identity Services within the browser context. The SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the token, maps it to the user's identity & native session, and establishes a regular web session – setting the standard browser session cookies. From there, the accessed target web application authenticates against SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication transparently via its standard SAML or OIDC flow reusing the established IAS session with no user interaction required.

The key properties of this approach:

  • Security-friendly: The SSO token is a one-time use token with a short validity. There are no long-lived session cookies involved on the identity provider side.
  • Admin-controlled: The feature can be controlled via a feature flag in SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit of the Joule Work mobile app. The administrator also defines exactly which web applications are trusted for this flow by configuring them in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.
  • Broadly compatible: The feature works with all in-app browser variants offered by Joule Work mobile app on iOS and Android. It also works with all web applications – SAP solutions as well as custom or third-party apps – as long as they share a common SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication tenant with the environment the mobile app is onboarded to.

Key Prerequisite: SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as the Common Trust Anchor

For the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow to work, SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication must be the identity provider for both the native mobile application and all web applications that should benefit from seamless SSO. The trust is established and maintained through SAP Cloud Identity Services, making it the single common denominator across all participants.

It is important to clarify that SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication does not need to be your end users' primary authentication system. In many enterprise landscapes, SAP Cloud Identity Services acts as an identity proxy, delegating the actual authentication to a corporate identity provider such as Microsoft Entra ID or other identity providers. This is fully supported. The only requirement is that SAP Cloud Identity Services is the first common trust anchor in the chain – meaning all participating web applications are configured to trust it directly, even if it in turn proxies to a corporate IdP.

One configuration detail to be aware of in proxy setups: the "Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP" setting in SAP Cloud Identity Services must not be enabled for the applications involved in the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow. When this setting is active, SAP Cloud Identity Services bypasses its own session handling and always redirects to the corporate IdP – which would cause the Mobile-to-Web SSO token to be ignored, and users would end up with a login prompt again. Beyond Mobile-to-Web SSO, enabling this setting can also cause issues in iFrame-based in-place app integration scenarios, as corporate identity providers frequently block iFraming to protect against clickjacking and similar attack vectors. For more information on this setting, see Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP (SAP Help).

The trust for the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow is configured explicitly in SAP Cloud Identity Services. The administrator defines which web application entries in SAP Cloud Identity Services are allowed to participate in this flow – only applications that have been explicitly added to the trust configuration can benefit from it. In addition, SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the URL of the target web application against the allowed redirect URIs configured for the trusted application. This means a web application can only receive a seamless SSO session if its URL matches one of the permitted redirect URI patterns. Both aspects – the application trust list and the redirect URI validation – ensure that the administrator retains full control over which applications can be reached via this mechanism.

Please note: Customers with SAP Build Work Zone subscriptions created before March 2025 may not yet be using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication directly. For these environments, it is necessary to explicitly switch the authentication mechanism. Subscriptions created after that date are automatically set up with SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. Please refer to the SAP Help documentation for the migration steps: Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.

Pro tip: As with other features that depend on shared sessions and cookies – such as the iFrame-based in-place integration of web applications – it is important that all components share a common super domain. We strongly recommend operating SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication and your SAP cloud solutions under the SAP Common Super Domain cloud.sap. If you are not yet familiar with this topic, our earlier blog post explains the background and the configuration steps in detail: Demystifying the Common Super Domain for SAP Mobile Start.

Which Web Applications Are Covered Automatically?

When Joule Work mobile app is connected to SAP Build Work Zone, both share the same Application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. This means that SAP Build Work Zone itself is automatically within the trust scope – no additional configuration is needed for it.

This automatic coverage includes for example:

  • SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition Workspaces – collaborative workspace content embedded in the Work Zone experience
  • Custom extensions hosted on SAP BTP – applications built for example with the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, deployed to the SAP BTP and served using the Approuter of SAP Build Work Zone. (For details on building such extensions, see Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start)
  • SAP Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition running through an SAP Cloud Connector using a Principal Propagation Destination – these are tunneled through the BTP subaccount and are therefore covered by the same Application entry

The key distinction is that automatic coverage applies to applications that are part of – or tunneled through – the SAP BTP subaccount and its corresponding SAP Cloud Identity Services Application. Applications that run on their own infrastructure and have their own independent trust relationship with SAP Cloud Identity Services are not automatically covered, regardless of whether they are launched in embedded ("in-place") mode or opened directly ("ex-place"). A prominent example is SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: even when it is iFramed within the SAP Build Work Zone shell, it has its own Application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services and must be explicitly added to the Native-Web Trust group.

For all such web applications – SAP solutions with their own IAS Application entry, third-party apps, or any app not part of the Work Zone BTP subaccount – administrators add them to the trust configuration in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as described in the setup steps below.

Administrator Setup

Step 1: Verify That IAS-Based Authentication Is Active

Before enabling the feature, confirm that SAP Build Work Zone is using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as its identity provider. If this is not yet the case, follow the migration steps referenced in the prerequisite section above.

Step 2: Enable the Feature Flag in SAP Mobile Services

In the SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit, navigate to the Client Settings. In the Feature Flags section, locate the entry named "MobiletoWebSingleSign-On" and activate it.

Screenshot showing the highlighted feature flag in the activated state in the Mobile Services Admin Cockpit.

For reference, the corresponding documentation can be found here: Single Sign-On for Web Apps – SAP Help

Step 3: Add Trusted Web Applications in SAP Cloud Identity Services

For web applications that are not automatically covered through the SAP Build Work Zone application (see the previous section), you can add them to the trust configuration:

  1. Sign in to the administration console for SAP Cloud Identity Services.
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Applications.
  3. Select the application entry that corresponds to your SAP Build Work Zone instance.
  4. Go to the Trust tab, then navigate to Application APIs → Dependencies → Native-Web Trust.
  5. Click Add and select the application with which you want to establish trust.
  6. Save your selection.

Screenshot of SAP Cloud Identity Services to maintain Native-Web Trust Settings

Users opening applications from this trust list in the in-app browser will now benefit from the seamless Mobile-to-Web SSO flow.

Step 4: Verify the Redirect URIs of Trusted Applications

As described in the prerequisite section, SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the URL of the target web application against the allowed redirect URIs of the trusted application entry. For SAP-delivered solutions – such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – the redirect URIs are typically pre-configured correctly by SAP and no further action is needed.

However, for custom-built applications or third-party web apps that rely on the general SAP Business Technology Platform application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services, you should verify that the application's URL is covered by the configured redirect URI patterns. If the URL does not match any permitted pattern, the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow will not complete successfully for that application.

You can review and adjust the redirect URIs in the SAP Cloud Identity Services administration console under the application's configuration. For the rules and wildcard syntax supported for redirect URIs, refer to the SAP Help documentation: Redirect URIs and Post Logout Redirect URI Rules (SAP Help).

Summary

With the Mobile-to-Web SSO feature, Joule Work mobile app version 3.0 closes the session gap between the native app context and the in-app browser – cleanly, securely, and with full administrator control.

Key takeaways:

  • The feature eliminates the need for long-lived "Remember me" cookies on the identity provider, which are a security concern and not universally available.
  • It is based on short-lived, one-time use SSO tokens issued and validated by SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication, which acts as the common trust anchor.
  • The feature is optional and in control by the customer: an administrator (de-/) activates it via a feature flag in SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit of Joule Work mobile app and controls which web applications participate via the Native-Web Trust configuration in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.
  • SAP Build Work Zone web UIs and tunneled embedded business apps are automatically covered. Additional apps can be added with a few clicks.
  • The feature is compatible with all in-app browser variants in Joule Work mobile app and all trusted web applications, regardless of whether they are SAP solutions or custom apps.

If you need further assistance configuring and making use of this new feature in the Joule Work mobile app, feel free to contact our product management team using jouleworkmobile@sap.com, which will be able to suggest the next steps for your scenario.

Further Reading

  • Joule Work mobile app – Single Sign-On for Web Apps (SAP Help)
  • Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (SAP Help)
  • Demystifying the Common Super Domain for SAP Mobile Start (SAP Community Blog)
  • Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start – Leveraging Mobile Native Features (SAP Community Blog)
  • SAP Mobile Experience Community Page

entry.title: CX Onboarding - New Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Playlist on AI
entry.content:
New Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Playlist on AI: How AI Is Transforming Customer Experience

The business world is changing fast, and customer expectations are higher than ever. Today, customers expect seamless, personalized experiences at every touchpoint—and artificial intelligence is no longer a nice-to-have, but a fundamental capability to deliver them.

AI is transforming how organizations operate by enabling them to better understand customer behavior, anticipate needs, and respond with precision. What was once reactive and manual is becoming proactive and intelligent, allowing businesses to move faster, make better decisions, and create more meaningful connections with their customers.

At SAP, this transformation goes beyond isolated AI use cases. It is part of a broader vision: the Autonomous Enterprise. In this model, end-to-end business processes are powered by AI and organized across core functions such as Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, Human Capital Management, and Customer Experience. AI agents operate across these domains, executing complex workflows autonomously while maintaining enterprise governance and control.

Within this vision, Customer Experience plays a central role. Autonomous CX brings together CRM and customer experience processes into a unified system, where AI acts on a complete, real-time view of your business. This enables organizations to deliver consistent, intelligent, and highly personalized experiences at scale.

This transformation becomes tangible in solutions like SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2, where AI is embedded directly into everyday workflows.

In customer service, AI helps teams move from information overload to instant expertise. Capabilities such as case management assistance streamline case preparation, processing, and resolution while learning from every interaction—turning expert knowledge into reusable insights for the entire team.

In sales, AI enables a shift from data overload to guided selling. Intelligent assistants help prioritize opportunities, identify risks early, and automate time-consuming tasks like quote generation, allowing sales teams to focus on what matters most: closing deals and building relationships.

Together, these capabilities represent a fundamental shift in how Customer Experience is delivered in both efficiency and better outcomes for customers.

As AI adoption continues to accelerate, many organizations are looking for practical ways to get started and realize value quickly—especially during onboarding and implementation phases.

To support our customers on this journey, the CX Onboarding team has created a 📺comprehensive video playlist for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2. This playlist is designed to introduce the key AI capabilities available within the solutions and help you accelerate adoption, maximize ROI, and build a strong foundation for success.

👇Read below to learn more.

Introduction to SAP Business AI

Learn why AI is more important than ever and about SAP’s integrated approach to Customer Experience and intelligent applications. It shows how SAP connects CX with backend systems through a unified data layer, enabling real-time insights and seamless collaboration across business functions.

Joule, SAP’s Business Copilot

Get introduced to Joule, SAP’s AI copilot, and how it enables natural, conversational interaction with business systems. Joule helps users access information quickly, automate tasks, and navigate to the right applications, all while integrating with tools like Microsoft 365.

AI Agents for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This video explains what AI agents are and how they operate autonomously to perform tasks with minimal human intervention. It showcases use cases such as automatic case classification and a Q&A agent that improves response accuracy and consistency.

Generative AI Features for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This session focuses on generative AI and how it creates new content based on learned patterns. It demonstrates features such as Lead Booster, which enhances customer understanding, and the Email Draft Recommender, which helps streamline communication.

Machine Learning Features for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

Explore how machine learning helps systems learn from data and improve over time. Become familiar with its capabilities such as Lead Intelligence, which predicts conversion likelihood, and Case Topic Analyzer, which organizes case data into meaningful insights.

🔍Explore our playlist to learn how to make the most out of AI with Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you for reading this blog. We hope you found the content insightful. Please give us a like or leave a comment; we’d love to hear from you!

You can request a one‑on‑one call with one of our onboarding advisors using this form. 

💻Want to get in touch with us? Send an email to SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

👉Key Resources

  • Onboarding Resource Center: SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2
  • AI guide for Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2
  • Joule Agents and Joule Assistants for customer experience
  • SAP Business AI in the Discovery Center

entry.title: Enhance SAP Joule for Consultants with Custom Knowledge Grounding
entry.content:
Enhance SAP Joule for Consultants with Custom Knowledge Grounding

Table of Contents

  • 1. Introduction
    • 1.1 Overview
    • 1.2 Recommendations
  • 2. Data Repositories
    • 2.1 Data Repositories - Overview
    • 2.2 Object Store
    • 2.3 Microsoft SharePoint
    • 2.4 SAP Build Work Zone
  • 3. AI Core Document Grounding
    • 3.1 AI Core Document Grounding - Overview
    • 3.2 AI Core instance creation
    • 3.3 AI Core Document Grounding with Bruno
    • 3.4 AI Core Document Grounding with AI Launchpad
    • 3.5 Support
  • 4. Connection to SAP Joule for Consultants
    • 4.1 Destination Service setup
    • 4.2 Admin Console configuration
  • 5. Testing
    • 5.1 Testing Custom Knowledge Grounding

1. Introduction

1.1 Overview

1.1 a) What Custom Knowledge Grounding Does

Custom Knowledge Grounding extends SAP Joule for Consultants with your organization's own documents. The documents are uploaded to a data repository, indexed by SAP AI Core Document Grounding, and retrieved at runtime to ground SAP Joule for Consultants' answers. Users get responses that combine the SAP-curated content SAP Joule for Consultants ships with and their firm's own methodologies, policies, and delivery standards.

1.1 b) Prerequisites

  • SAP Joule for Consultants — an existing instance. See Onboarding Guide for SAP Joule for Consultants | SAP Help Portal.
  • SAP AI Core with the extended plan — required by Document Grounding. See SAP AI Core — SAP Discovery Center.
  • Object Store (recommended for the initial setup) — the simplest data repository to start with, and the example used throughout this guide. See Object Store — SAP Discovery Center.

1.1 c) Pricing

Custom Knowledge Grounding relies on paid SAP services that are billed separately from SAP Joule for Consultants:

  • SAP AI Core (extended plan) is mandatory. Charges accrue per document indexed and per token retrieved — see the Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core Calculator and Pricing.
  • Object Store (standard plan) if you take the recommended setup path. See Pricing.
  • SAP AI Launchpad (standard plan) is optional — needed only if you prefer the UI-driven setup over the REST API path. See Pricing.

Review the pricing of each service before subscribing.

1.1 d) What This Guide Covers

The remaining sections walk through the end-to-end setup:

  • 2. Data Repositories — pick a supported data repository (e.g., Object Store, Microsoft SharePoint, or SAP Build Work Zone) and upload your documents to it.
  • 3. AI Core Document Grounding — provision SAP AI Core with the extended plan, then create a pipeline that ingests, chunks, embeds, and indexes the documents.
  • 4. Connection to SAP Joule for Consultants — configure a Destination Service instance, then enable Custom Knowledge Grounding in the SAP Joule for Consultants Console and connect it to the pipeline.
  • 5. Testing — verify the setup by asking SAP Joule for Consultants a question whose answer is only available in your uploaded documents.
Note: Depending on what is already in place — an existing AI Core Document Grounding pipeline, ready-to-use data repository credentials, or neither — you may be able to skip directly to a later section. The next section, 1.2 Recommendations, opens with a decision tree for choosing the right entry point and then covers data repository choice and subaccount layout.

1.2 Recommendations

1.2 a) Where to Start

Not every reader has to work through every section of this guide. Use the decision tree below to pick the entry point that matches what you already have in place:

  • An AI Core Document Grounding pipeline is already provisioned (resource group, generic secret, and a pipeline reporting FINISHED against the data repository you intend to use) → skip ahead to the 4. Connection to SAP Joule for Consultants section, starting with 4.1 Destination Service setup. Configure the Destination Service against the existing pipeline and enable Custom Knowledge Grounding in the SAP Joule for Consultants Console.
  • The data repository credentials are already available (e.g., a SharePoint site with the Entra app registered, an S3 bucket, or a Work Zone tenant with Document Grounding enabled) and you have run an AI Core Document Grounding setup before → skip the 2. Data Repositories section and start with the 3. AI Core Document Grounding section.
  • Neither of the above applies → start at the 2. Data Repositories section with 2.2 Object Store, as described below. It is the fastest path to a working setup and the one the rest of this guide uses as the running example.

1.2 b) Start with Object Store

Custom Knowledge Grounding can read from any of the supported data repositories. For the initial setup, Object Store is the recommended choice:

  • Fast activation — the Object Store instance is provisioned by a booster and ready in minutes. SharePoint, customer-managed S3, and Work Zone setups often take longer than anticipated because of onboarding complications, approval, and security processes.
  • Very low cost — Object Store charges are small. Even when run for a few months until the target data repository is ready, the extra cost is negligible — see Pricing.
  • Reversible — once the target data repository is online and indexed, point Custom Knowledge Grounding at the new pipeline and decommission the Object Store instance.

The key advantage is that Custom Knowledge Grounding can be live in SAP Joule for Consultants while the longer-running setup of your target data repository runs in parallel — instead of blocking the entire rollout on it.

Note: The Object Store instance must be provisioned in an AWS-based subaccount, since AI Core Document Grounding consumes it through the AWS S3 API.
Note: Answers themselves are generated the same way regardless of the data repository. Source citations are clickable for SharePoint and Work Zone — they link back to the original file. For Object Store and the other repositories, citations show the document name and page reference but do not resolve to the file.

1.2 c) Subaccount Layout and Existing Instances

The four moving parts — Object Store (or any other data repository), SAP AI Core, Destination Service, and SAP Joule for Consultants — communicate through service-key credentials, which allows for very flexible placement: they can all sit in the same subaccount, in different subaccounts, or even in different global accounts.

  • Reuse existing instances where available. If your organization already runs SAP AI Core (with the extended plan) or SAP AI Launchpad in another subaccount, you can reuse them instead of provisioning new instances.
  • The Destination Service can be deployed in the SAP Joule for Consultants subaccount or in the AI Core subaccount. In general, it is recommended to place it in the same subaccount as the consuming application—in this case, the SAP Joule for Consultants subaccount.
  • The Object Store must live in an AWS-based subaccount (see above). The SAP Joule for Consultants subaccount is a convenient option, since it runs on AWS data centers.

2. Data Repositories

2.1 Data Repositories - Overview

2.1 a) What a Data Repository Provides

AI Core Document Grounding does not host the source documents itself — it reads them from an external data repository and serves the resulting index to SAP Joule for Consultants. The data repository is the system of record where you upload and maintain the documents you want to ground SAP Joule for Consultants' answers on.

The starting point for the AI Core Document Grounding documentation is Grounding | SAP Help Portal.

2.1 b) Supported Data Repositories

AI Core Document Grounding can read content from the following data repositories:

  • Microsoft SharePoint
  • AWS S3
  • SFTP
  • SAP Build Work Zone
  • SAP Document Management service
  • ServiceNow
  • Google Drive

Setup steps for Google Drive, Microsoft SharePoint, SAP Build Work Zone, and ServiceNow (SNOW) are described on Set Up Data Repository Integration | SAP Help Portal.

Note: That help page is written for Document Grounding with SAP Joule for Business. Only the data repository setup instructions apply here — the remaining content on the page is specific to SAP Joule for Business and is not relevant for SAP Joule for Consultants.

The next subsections walk through the setup of three data repositories in more detail:

  • 2.2 Object Store
  • 2.3 Microsoft SharePoint
  • 2.4 SAP Build Work Zone
