This creates a two-step flow where you browse categories in the carousel, then drill into products for a selected category in a list.

# northwind_category_carousel/functions/get_categories_carousel.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Categories?$select=CategoryID,CategoryName,Description"
        result_variable: api_result
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: categories
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: message
        scripting_type: handlebars
        message: >
          {
            "type": "carousel",
            "delay": 0,
            "content": {
              "enableDetailView": false,
              "cards": [
                {{#eachJoin categories}}
                  {
                    "title": "{{CategoryName}}",
                    "subtitle": "{{Description}}",
                    "imageUrl": "sap-icon://product",
                    "buttons": [
                      {
                        "type": "postback",
                        "title": "Explore {{CategoryName}}",
                        "text": "Explore {{CategoryName}}",
                        "value": "List products in category {{CategoryID}}"
                      }
                    ]
                  }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
            }
          }

The postback button's value field, "List products in category {{CategoryID}}", is the utterance that Joule receives when the user clicks "Explore Beverages". The list capability's scenario description includes "list products in a specific category" and declares category_id as a slot, so Joule picks it up and extracts the numeric ID from that utterance. The list function then applies $filter=CategoryID%20eq%20<? category_id ?> to the OData request.

Message Type: Carousel

When to use: a small, fixed set of entities where side-by-side comparison is useful and each item has a natural follow-on action, such as drilling into a filtered view.

 

Media Messages

The media type embeds an image or video directly in the chat window. For video, Joule supports YouTube, Vimeo, Daily Motion, SAP Video, and SAP TV Video URLs. For direct file links, supported formats are MP4, WEBM, OGV, and 3GP. All URLs must be publicly accessible.

The sample capability for this section is intentionally simple, with no API call, no parameters, and no slots. It demonstrates that a capability does not need to reach a backend service to be useful. The function sends a short text message followed by the video embed in a single unconditional action group.

# sapphire_2026_media/functions/get_video.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "Here's the SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 keynote."
      - type: message
        message:
          type: media
          content: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CocpyxAizwE"

The two message actions in the same action group send back-to-back. The text message appears first, giving the embed context before the video player renders below it. This chaining pattern applies to any message type combination: you can follow a card with a buttons message, or a text summary with a list of results, by placing multiple message actions in the same action group.

The content field takes the URL directly as a string without additional nesting required. For a YouTube URL, Joule renders an embedded player. The user can play the video without leaving the chat.

Message Type: Media

When to use: product demo videos, how-to recordings, keynote highlights, or any response where a visual or audio asset communicates more than text alone.

 

Buttons Messages

The buttons type renders one or more clickable action buttons, optionally preceded by a summary text. It is the right choice when you want to give the user a clear set of next steps directly in the response, or when a single result can be explored in multiple ways.

This capability is the launcher for the rest of the post. Ask Joule to "show me different Joule message types in action" and it renders one postback button per message type. Clicking a button sends the right prompt back to Joule and triggers that capability without the user having to type anything.

# northwind_product_buttons/functions/show_message_type_launcher.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: buttons
          content:
            title: "Pick a message type to see it in action:"
            buttons:
              - type: postback
                title: See as text
                value: "Show me product Chai as text"
              - type: postback
                title: See as card
                value: "Show me product Chai as a card"
              - type: postback
                title: Browse all products via list
                value: "Provide me a list of products"
              - type: postback
                title: See categories as carousel
                value: "Show me Northwind categories"
              - type: postback
                title: See products as integration card
                value: "Show me products as an integration card"
              - type: postback
                title: See as illustrated message
                value: "Show me product Chai as an illustrated message"
              - type: postback
                title: See as media (SAPPHIRE video)
                value: "What happened at SAPPHIRE 2026"
              - type: postback
                title: Rate Joule (Likert)
                value: "Rate my Joule experience"

The function has no parameters, no slots, and no API call. Chai is hardcoded in each postback value that requires a product name, so every downstream capability receives a valid input when the button is clicked.

Some button types available in Joule:

  • postback: sends the value string back to Joule as a new user message, continuing the conversation
  • navigation: opens a URL in the browser or navigates within the host application
  • clientdata: passes a silent payload to the host application without sending a visible message

Message Type: Buttons

When to use: after delivering a result when the logical next action is predictable, or when a single entity can be viewed in multiple ways and you want to let the user choose without typing follow-up prompts.

 

Likert Scale Messages

The likert_scale type renders a row of labeled, clickable scale options directly in the chat. It is designed for in-chat surveys and feedback collection where you need a numeric rating on a defined scale. When the user clicks an option, the selected value is stored in a slot and becomes available to downstream steps in the same dialog function.

The supported range is 1 to 7 options. The compiler does not enforce contiguous values or prevent duplicates, which gives you flexibility to configure non-standard scales. In practice a simple 1-to-5 or 1-to-7 Likert scale is the most common use case.

The sample capability asks the user to rate how well Joule fits their needs, stores the response in a slot, and sends a personalised thank-you text message.

The scenario declares the slot so Joule knows to wait for the Likert response before advancing:

# northwind_product_likert/scenarios/joule_satisfaction.yaml
description: >-
  Capture the user's satisfaction with Joule. Use this when the user
  wants to rate their experience or answer a survey question about Joule.

slots:
  - name: joule_fit_for_purpose
    description: >-
      The user's rating of whether Joule does what they need, on a scale
      from 1 (Strongly disagree) to 5 (Strongly agree).

target:
  type: function
  name: scenario_joule_survey

The function sends the scale first, then branches on the captured slot value:

# northwind_product_likert/functions/scenario_joule_survey.yaml (relevant section)
action_groups:
  - condition: joule_fit_for_purpose == null
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: likert_scale
          content:
            prompt: "Joule does what I need it to do"
            options:
              - label: "Strongly disagree"
                value: 1
              - label: "Disagree"
                value: 2
              - label: "Neutral"
                value: 3
              - label: "Agree"
                value: 4
              - label: "Strongly agree"
                value: 5
  - condition: joule_fit_for_purpose != null
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "Thank you! You rated Joule's fit for purpose as <? joule_fit_for_purpose ?> out of 5."

The first action group fires when the slot is empty, displaying the scale. Once the user clicks an option, the slot is populated and Joule re-enters the function, this time matching the second action group and sending the confirmation text.

A few practical notes:

  • The likert_scale message type is designed to work with slot filling. Declare the slot in the scenario so Joule waits for the user's selection before proceeding.
  • The prompt field is the question text displayed above the scale. Keep it short, one sentence.
  • options requires at least one entry and at most seven. Values do not need to be contiguous.
  • The selected value is stored as a number in the slot, so downstream SpEL comparisons use numeric operators, such as joule_fit_for_purpose >= 4.

Message Type: Likert Scale

When to use: in-chat feedback forms, satisfaction surveys, or any scenario where you need a structured numeric rating from the user without leaving the chat.

 

UI Integration Cards

How it differs from a plain card message

The card message type is a fixed Joule UI component with a predefined layout. It has a title, subtitle, image, status, and description. You populate fields but you cannot change the layout itself.

The ui5integrationCard type embeds a full UI5 Integration Card driven by a JSON manifest. You control the card type, the column layout, field grouping, labels, and semantic coloring. The trade-off is verbosity: the manifest is considerably longer than a card message, and all data must come from the dialog function. The card manifest cannot reach out to a BTP destination directly.

Card types available

The UI5 Integration Card framework supports a range of card types, each suited to different data shapes and presentation needs:

Card typeBest for
ObjectSingle-record detail view with grouped label/value fields
TableMulti-record tabular display with columns, alignment, and state badges
ListCompact vertically stacked items with icon, title, description
AnalyticalCharts: bar, line, donut, bullet, stacked bar
TimelineChronological event sequences
CalendarDate-based scheduling views
ComponentCustom UI5 component embed (not supported in Joule)

The full catalog with live examples is in the UI5 Integration Card Explorer.

The Table card

The Table card is well suited for multi-record results. It renders columns with headers, supports right-alignment for numeric values, and displays semantic state badges with icons on any column using state, showStateIcon, and inverted.

The sample capability fetches up to 10 products with their category name by extending the Category entity, as well as the stock status, and renders them as a table with four columns: Product, Category, In Stock, and Status.

The key pattern is a two-step construction:

  1. Build the rows array with Handlebars, computing status and statusState per row inline using {{#if}} comparisons against UnitsInStock and Discontinued
  2. Inject the rows into the manifest via SpEL and send it as a message action
# northwind_product_integration_card/functions/get_product_integration_card.yaml
# ... (parameters and api-request action groups follow the same pattern as earlier examples)
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: products
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: set-variables
        scripting_type: handlebars
        variables:
          - name: tableRows
            value: >
              [
                {{#eachJoin products}}
                {
                  "product": "{{ProductName}}",
                  "category": "{{Category.CategoryName}}",
                  "unitsInStock": "{{UnitsInStock}}",
                  "status": "{{#if Discontinued}}Discontinued{{else if (eq UnitsInStock 0)}}Out of Stock{{else if (lt UnitsInStock 10)}}Low Stock{{else}}In Stock{{/if}}",
                  "statusState": "{{#if Discontinued}}Error{{else if (eq UnitsInStock 0)}}Error{{else if (lt UnitsInStock 10)}}Warning{{else}}Success{{/if}}"
                }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
      - type: message
        scripting_type: spel
        message: >
          {
            "type": "ui5integrationCard",
            "content": {
              "_version": "1.17.0",
              "sap.app": { "type": "card", "id": "northwind.product.tablecard" },
              "sap.card": {
                "type": "Table",
                "header": {
                  "title": "Northwind Products",
                  "subtitle": "Showing up to 10 products"
                },
                "content": {
                  "data": { "json": <? tableRows ?> },
                  "maxItems": 10,
                  "row": {
                    "columns": [
                      { "title": "Product",   "value": "{product}",      "identifier": true },
                      { "title": "Category",  "value": "{category}" },
                      { "title": "In Stock",  "value": "{unitsInStock}", "hAlign": "End" },
                      {
                        "title": "Status",
                        "value": "{status}",
                        "state": "{statusState}",
                        "showStateIcon": true,
                        "inverted": true
                      }
                    ]
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }

A few points worth noting:

  • $expand=Category fetches the related Category entity in a single OData call.
  • data.json placement: in Table cards, data sits inside content, not at the sap.card level. The row.columns then use {product}, {category} etc. as binding paths into the JSON array.
  • State badges: "state": "{statusState}" with "showStateIcon": true and "inverted": true produces the filled colored badge with icon seen in the UI5 Card Explorer examples. Valid state values are Success, Warning, Error, Information, and None.
  • Two-step construction: Handlebars builds tableRows as a JSON array string, then SpEL injects it into the manifest via <? tableRows ?>. This is necessary because the YAML schema validator rejects SpEL expressions in positions that expect a literal array.

Message Type: UI Integration Card

When to use: multi-record results where column layout, numeric alignment, or semantic state coloring adds clarity that a plain list message cannot provide. For a single-record detail view, the Object card type is a better fit. The full range of UI5 card types, Analytical charts, Timeline, Calendar, opens up significant display possibilities beyond what Joule's built-in message types support.

Key restrictions for ui5integrationCard in Joule:

  • Only static data bindings are supported. You cannot declare a BTP destination inside the manifest and have Joule fetch data from it at render time.
  • Action handlers defined in the manifest are not executed by Joule.
  • Component card types are not supported.
  • Filters and user-defined manifest parameters are not supported.

 

Illustrated Messages

The illustrated_message type renders an SAP illustration alongside a title, subtitle, and up to two call-to-action buttons. It is designed for the moments that bookend a transaction or search, such as a success confirmation, a not-found state, or a system error. Rather than showing a blank text response after an action, it gives the user a visual signal that something meaningful happened.

The sample capability looks up a Northwind product and branches into two states based on what the API returns:

  • Product found, in stock, and not discontinued: Success illustration
  • Product found but discontinued or out of stock: UnableToLoad illustration
  • Product not found at all: tntEmpty illustration
# northwind_product_illustrated/functions/get_product_illustrated.yaml (relevant section)
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: product
            value: <? api_result.body.value[0] ?>
      - type: message
        message:
          type: illustrated_message
          content:
            illustration_name: "<? (product.Discontinued == true || product.UnitsInStock == 0) ? 'UnableToLoad' : 'TntSuccess' ?>"
            title: "<? product.ProductName ?>"
            subtitle: "$<? product.UnitPrice ?> · <? product.UnitsInStock ?> units in stock"
            buttons:
              - title: Look up another product
                type: postback
                text: Look up another product
                value: "Show me another product as an illustrated message"
              - title: Browse catalog
                type: navigation
                navigation_target:
                  url: "https://services.odata.org/V3/Northwind/Northwind.svc/Products"
  - condition: api_result.status_code != 200 || api_result.body.value == null || api_result.body.value.size() == 0
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: illustrated_message
          content:
            illustration_name: NoEntries
            title: "Product not found"
            subtitle: "No product matching '<? product_name ?>' was found in the Northwind catalog."
            buttons:
              - title: Try again
                type: postback
                text: Try again
                value: "Show me another product as an illustrated message"

The illustration_name is a SpEL expression that evaluates to UnableToLoad when the product is discontinued or out of stock, and TntSuccess otherwise. The not-found branch uses the separate NoEntries illustrated message.

The buttons array supports the same button types as the card and buttons message types: postback, navigation, phonenumber, and clientdata. The postback button's value field is the utterance Joule receives when the user clicks. Note it includes "as an illustrated message" to keep Joule routing to this capability rather than a different one for the next lookup.

A few practical notes:

  • For inspiration on illustration names, browse the UI5 Web Components IllustratedMessageType. To see how they look, check the UI5 IllustratedMessage samples.
  • The maximum number of buttons is two.

Message Type: Illustrated Message

When to use: after a mutation (create, update, delete) to confirm the outcome, or when a search returns no results and you want to guide the user toward a recovery action rather than leaving them with a silent empty state.

 

Common Pitfalls

A few mistakes that are easy to make when building capabilities for the first time:

  • URL-encode dynamic values in OData paths. Spaces in a user-supplied slot value (e.g. "Chai Tea") will break an OData filter if embedded directly. Add a set-variables step first with <? product_name.urlEncode() ?>, then reference the encoded variable in the path.
  • Use {{#eachJoin}} instead of {{#each}} when building JSON arrays in Handlebars. {{#eachJoin}} inserts commas between items automatically. {{#each}} does not, which produces invalid JSON.
  • In Table integration cards, data belongs inside content, not at the sap.card level. Placing it at the wrong level causes the card to render empty.
  • likert_scale stores the selected value as a number. Downstream SpEL comparisons must use numeric operators (>=, ==), not string comparisons.
  • Postback button value strings must match the target capability's scenario description. Joule uses the value as an utterance for intent matching. Include the keywords that route to the right capability.

 

UI Constraints and Limits

Each message type enforces hard limits on element counts and text lengths. Exceeding these silently truncates or hides content. The full tables covering element counts, character limits, full-screen widths, and markdown variants are in the UI Constraints and Limits page of the Joule Development Guide.

 

Try It Yourself

The complete sample files are in this GitHub folder. The assistant is structured as one Digital Assistant file referencing nine separate capability folders, one per message type. Each capability's scenario description includes a phrase like "as text", "as a card", or "as a list". Joule then uses these during intent matching to route the incoming prompt to the correct capability. Once deployed, ask for any Northwind product with buttons to get a clickable launcher that triggers each message type without typing the prompts manually.

Deploy everything in one step:

cd Blog_01_joule_message_types
joule deploy -c -n "northwind_message_types"
joule launch "northwind_message_types"

Try each of these prompts to exercise all nine message types:

Message typeSample prompt
Text"Show me product Chai as text"
Card"Show me product Chai as a card"
Buttons"Show me different Joule message types in action"
List"Provide me a list of products"
Carousel"Show me Northwind categories"
UI Integration Card"Show me products as an integration card"
Illustrated Message"Show me product Chai as an illustrated message"
Likert Scale"Rate your Joule experience"
Media"Show me the SAPPHIRE 2026 keynote"

For the carousel, click the "Explore" button on any category card to trigger the list capability filtered by that category. The list capability also works standalone. "List products in category 1" will return only Beverages products directly.

For the illustrated message, try a product that exists ("Show me product Chai as an illustrated message") to see the success state, and a name that matches nothing ("Show me product XYZ as an illustrated message") to see the not-found empty state.

What is Next

In the next post we look at restricting which scenarios are visible to which users using IAS group membership and BTP role assignments. See Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Role-Based Scenario Access in Joule. More posts in this series are on the way.

Resources

  • Messages — Joule Development Guide
  • UI Integration Cards — Joule Development Guide
  • UI5 Integration Card Explorer

entry.title: New Course: Discovering the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
🚀New Learning Opportunity: Discovering the Autonomous Enterprise

As organizations continue to navigate rapid digital transformation, the concept of the autonomous enterprise is quickly becoming a key driver of innovation, efficiency, and resilience. This course is designed to help you understand what that means and why it matters.

🌐What Is an Autonomous Enterprise?

An autonomous enterprise leverages advanced technologies such as AI, machine learning, and intelligent automation to run and optimize business processes with minimal human intervention. The goal? Faster decisions, improved efficiency, and the ability to continuously adapt in a changing world.

📚What You’ll Learn

In this course, you’ll gain:

  • A clear understanding of SAP's autonomous enterprise vision
  • Insights into how intelligent technologies transform business operations
  • Real-world examples of how automation and data-driven decision-making create value
  • An overview of the building blocks and key capabilities needed to get started

Whether you're new to the topic or looking to deepen your understanding, this course provides a solid foundation.

👥Who Should Take This Course?

This learning experience is designed for:

  • Business professionals exploring digital transformation
  • IT leaders and architects
  • SAP customers, partners, and consultants
  • Anyone curious about the future of intelligent enterprises

No advanced technical background is required, just curiosity and a willingness to learn.

💡Why It Matters

The shift toward autonomous enterprises isn’t a distant future, it’s happening now. Organizations that embrace intelligent automation are better equipped to:

  • Respond faster to market changes
  • Improve operational efficiency
  • Unlock new business opportunities

This course helps you understand how to be part of that transformation.

✅Get Started Today

Take the first step toward understanding the future of business innovation.

👉  Discovering the Autonomous Enterprise

entry.title: Agentic Joule for developer: It’s all about YOUR CHOICE
entry.content:
At Sapphire, SAP made one theme impossible to miss: the future is agentic, the ERP is autonomous, and the SAP Business AI platform is turning into the foundation for how this intelligence gets delivered across the portfolio. Joule for Developers is part of that bigger story.

While some technology announcements feel like feature updates, this one feels more like a launch moment — the point where the plot twist. Over the past year, ABAP AI has been proving its value in the practical trenches: explaining old code, accelerating test work, helping with findings, and making cloud development easier. Useful? Absolutely. But still mostly assistive. What Sapphire makes clear is that the next chapter is far more ambitious: ABAP AI is evolving from a set of smart skills into an agent-led development model — one built around choice: your choice of your preferred IDE between ABAP Development Tools on Eclipse or on VSCode, your partner of choice with your preferred AI assistant and orchestration, your choice of ABAP system from BTP till any S/4HANA version from 2021 up and your choice of consumption. And on top a much more serious strategy for RISE custom code migration.

That is the real shift here. Not just “more AI,” but AI that starts behaving like a coordinated system.

So this blog is not about one more feature. This one is about a full shift of thinking, with the upcoming general availability of the ABAP MCP server, open support for partner assistants and orchestrators, the general availability of ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code, a new consumption-based commercial model, and major progress in custom code migration, SAP Joule for Developers is turning into a developer platform built around one central idea: your choice.

Agentic Joule for Developers arrives with the upcoming general availability of the ABAP MCP server and an open partner strategy built around your choice of assistants and orchestrators

The most exciting signal in this story is the ABAP MCP server as of this quarter: Q2 2026. It opens ABAP development to a wider agentic universe by exposing ABAP capabilities in a way that assistants and orchestrators can truly work with. This is where the story becomes more than assistance and starts to feel like momentum. Instead of one closed experience, SAP is shaping a future in which customers and partners can bring their preferred AI assistant, their preferred orchestration layer, and their preferred model strategy into the flow. The result is a far more elegant promise for ABAP teams: SAP provides the ABAP intelligence, the MCP layer becomes the bridge, and you decide how the full agentic experience comes together.

ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code completes the promise of real IDE choice for ABAP developers

Choice becomes real the moment it reaches the daily developer experience, and that is exactly why ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code matters so much. It turns SAP’s commitment to IDE freedom into something tangible. Teams can stay in the environment that feels natural to them while still tapping into the full power of agentic ABAP. In this new model, Visual Studio Code becomes the creative workspace, the MCP layer becomes the action engine, and partner orchestration brings the journey together from first intent to finished Fiori application. It is a smoother, more modern, more natural way to build. For the first release the new Visual Studio Code is focused on ABAP Cloud but by the end of the year with the promise of the roadmap, the entire ABAP Classic support will be extended from Eclipse to VSCode IDE also.

Meeting you where you are with ABAP AI across private cloud, public cloud, on premise, and BTP

One of the most expected announcement for Joule for developer was not on the features but on the reach: the customers stretch across private cloud, public cloud, BTP and either on premise systems and starting June, Joule for Developers is being positioned to meet our customers where they are, across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP BTP ABAP Environment, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition releases from 2021 onward. More than that for the RISE customers who fulfill the conditions, Joule for Developer will be available also on their On-Premise systems from 2021 onward. This changes the tone of adoption completely. Instead of waiting for every landscape decision to be perfect, teams can begin from the reality they already have and move forward from there.

Custom code migration steps into a new era with an agent-led toolchain for SAP S/4HANA transformation

If there is one place where agentic AI has to prove it can deliver more than clever assistance, it is custom code migration. And the CCM story now reads much more clearly as an end-to-end journey. It starts upstream with Cloud ALM, where project analysis helps teams understand the migration scope, structure the backlog, and identify where effort and risk are concentrated. That early project view matters because migration is never just a developer task. It is a transformation program. But once the scope is understood, the story shifts decisively into developer execution, and that is where the three CCM execution agents provided by Joule for developer come in.



1. Mass S/4 Custom Code Conversion Agent
This first agent planned for Q2 2026 is about scale. It helps developers take large volumes of custom code findings and move from assessment into executable remediation. Its role is to accelerate the mass conversion work that would otherwise remain trapped in analysis reports and manual follow-up.

2. Clean Core Adoption Agent
This second agent is about clean core transformation. It helps teams to move from technical correction to clean-core through recommendation and automatic fixes. This is planned for Q3 and Q4 2026.

3. Redesign and Modernization Agent (i.e. Dynpro-to-RAP)
This third and most complex agent is about application modernization and redesign. It focuses on the deeper transformation work, helping classic Dynpro-based patterns evolve toward a RAP-based future and turning modernization into a guided execution flow rather than a one-off rebuild effort. This agent is planned on the roadmap for late this year and early next year.

Together, these three agents create a much more structured CCM storyline: analyze the program in Cloud ALM, then execute the migration through conversion, clean-core adoption, and modernization. The timeline reinforces that progression. The first execution step begins in Q2 2026, broader execution capabilities expand through the second half of 2026, and the larger modernization promise continues to mature into 2027. What emerges is not just a better migration tool, but a much more credible migration motion from project analysis to developer delivery.

A new commercial model that lets you choose what you use, when you use it, and how far you scale it

Agentic Joule for developer is built as a choice-driven ecosystem also at the commercial layer. The model is designed for flexibility with consumption-based pricing, clear J4D activation, and differentiated paths for customers and partners. While the free promotion will end on 30th September the teams can work through ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse or VS Code, connect through the ABAP MCP Server and local ABAP MCP tools, at no extra cost, while taping into an agent pipeline powered by an open partner strategy at your choice that includes Microsoft, Amazon, Google, IBM, Anthropic and more. The commercial message is bold and unmistakable: Agentic Joule for developer is not just offering AI capabilities — it is giving customers and partners the freedom to build their own path, with their own tools, their own partners, and their own pace, paying only for the AI features which truly bring value to them. It feels far better suited to the rhythm of real AI adoption, where teams experiment, learn, expand, and refine over time rather than locking themselves into a single path on day one.

Bottom line: the future of ABAP looks less like a chatbot and more like an ecosystem

Put it all together and one message shines through powerfully: Agentic Joule for Developers is becoming a platform shaped around your choice. Your assistant. Your orchestrator. Your IDE. Your landscape. Your migration path. Your pace. And that is exactly what makes this new chapter feel so powerful: ABAP AI is growing up — from answering questions to orchestrating work, from isolated skills to partner-powered ecosystems, and from code assistance to full custom code migration strategy.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: Joule AI - Features and Capabilities in Report Management
entry.content:
Hello, SAP Community members!

Joule is the AI solution embedded in SAP Profitability and Performance Management (SAP PaPM) Cloud Standard Model, designed to help you interact with your reports. Instead of manually searching for data or navigating across report pages, you ask Joule - and it answers based on the full context of your report. 

This blog post is a comprehensive guide to all Joule features and capabilities available to end users. If you are looking for the technical integration and setup steps (SAP AI Core, SAP Launchpad, BTP Destination), please refer to our previous blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud SM: Enhancing Report Management with the Power of Generative AI Copilot – Joule 

In this post, we will walk through every feature available once Joule is set up: configuration settings, panel controls, conversation history, AI capabilities, file analysis, web search, inline editing, and using Joule without SAP AI Core. 

Without further ado, let's walk through each feature in detail.

Joule AI Settings (Administration) 

Before users can take full advantage of Joule, an administrator needs to configure the AI connection. Navigate to Administration → Settings, scroll to the Joule AI Settings section, and configure the following: 

  • Default SAP AI Core Destination Name - Select the SAP AI Core destination created in BTP. The dropdown lists all available BTP destinations in your subaccount. Choose the one configured for Joule with deployed AI models. 
  • Default AI Model - The model used for lightweight tasks: auto-generating conversation titles and producing follow-up question suggestions (quick replies). If no Reasoning AI Model is configured, this model also handles all user questions. 
  • Reasoning AI Model - When configured, this model takes over answering user questions, freeing the Default AI Model for title generation and quick reply suggestions only. Use a more capable model here for deeper analytical responses. 
  • Web Search - Toggle to activate internet search capabilities. When active, an additional AI model dropdown appears for selecting the model used for web search queries. This allows Joule to answer general questions beyond the report content. 

For detailed setup instructions and the list of supported AI models, see Joule AI Settings on the SAP Help Portal. 

Opening Joule in Reports 

Once Joule is configured, it becomes available on the Report Management screen. Look for the Joule icon in the header toolbar - click it to open the Joule conversation panel on the right side of the screen. 

You can start a conversation immediately - no need to save the report first. Joule understands the content of all pages in your current report, so you can ask questions about data from any page, regardless of which one you are viewing. 

The Joule Panel 

Conversation Controls 

Joule conversation panel offers several controls for managing your conversation: 

  • Stop Streaming - If Joule is generating a long response, click the Stop button located in the Joule message input area to interrupt it. Useful when you want to rephrase your question or take a different direction. 
  • View Conversation Options - Opens a menu with three actions: rename the current conversation, delete it, or start a new conversation. 
  • Close - Closes the Joule side panel and returns to the report view. 

Fullscreen Mode 

For a more spacious conversation experience, expand Joule to full screen using the Fullscreen icon in the Joule panel header. In full-screen mode, Joule takes over the entire screen - ideal for long conversations, reviewing file analysis results, or working with web search results. All panel features remain available in full-screen mode. Choose Exit Fullscreen to return to the side panel view. 

Follow-Up Questions 

After each response, Joule suggests three follow-up questions as clickable tiles. These quick replies allow you to continue the conversation without typing your next question. Choose a tile to send it instantly. 

Conversation History Sidebar 

Every conversation you start in Joule is automatically saved and accessible from the history sidebar. Choose the Menu icon (☰) in the Joule panel header to open it. 

Navigating Your Conversations 

The sidebar lists all your past conversations in chronological order, with the most recent at the top. The currently active conversation is highlighted. Conversations are personal - they are visible only to you, regardless of which environment or report you are in. The sidebar initially shows your 25 most recent conversations and loads earlier ones automatically as you scroll down. 

  • Select any conversation to open it and continue where you left off. 
  • The + New Conversation button at the top starts a fresh chat. 
  • Titles are generated automatically from your first message, making it easy to identify past topics. 

Rename and Delete Conversations 

To manage a conversation, hover over it in the sidebar (or use the three-dot menu in the active conversation header) to access the context menu: 

  • Rename - The conversation title becomes an inline editable text field (100-character limit). Type the new name and press Enter or click outside to save. Press Escape to cancel. A toast notification confirms the change. 
  • Delete - A confirmation dialog appears before the conversation is removed, preventing accidental deletions. 

Sidebar Settings and AI Notice 

At the bottom of the history sidebar, you will find two additional items: 

  • Settings - Opens a dialog with a single option: Turn off animations. Joule includes transition animations in several places - for example, on the welcome screen when opening Joule and in the history sidebar when displaying conversation titles. Toggle this if you prefer a static interface without transition animations. 
  • AI Notice - Opens the AI disclosure page, reminding users that Joule is powered by generative AI. All output should be reviewed before use, and sensitive personal data should not be entered. 

 

Joule Capabilities: What Can You Do? 

In Report Management, Joule offers more than conversational Q&A — it is a context-aware AI tool with several distinct capabilities: 

Analyzing Your Report 

The most fundamental capability: ask Joule any question about your report data, and it will analyze the content across all report pages to answer it. 

Example questions: 

  • "What is the total revenue for the top 10 customers? " 
  • "Summarize the key findings of this report." 
Asking About Charts 

Joule can interpret chart visualizations embedded in your report. Ask about trends, comparisons, top/bottom values, or calculations shown in the charts. 

Example questions: 

  • "Which customer has the highest revenue in this chart?" 
  • "What is the carbon footprint trend from Company-Vendor A?" 
Uploading Files for Analysis 

You can upload external files directly into the Joule conversation. Joule will read the file content and use it as additional context when answering your questions, in addition to the report data it already has. 

How to upload: 

  • Choose Upload File in the Joule message input area, or 
  • Drag and drop a file directly into the conversation input. 

File upload limits: 

  • Maximum 5 files per conversation 
  • Maximum 5 MB per file 
  • Supported file types: see Upload Files to Conversation on the SAP Help Portal. 

Note: 

  • Only the first non-empty sheet of an uploaded XLSX file is processed. If your data spans multiple sheets, consolidate it into a single sheet before uploading. 
  • Uploaded files are scanned for malware before being processed. 

Web Search 

When your administrator activates Web Search, Joule can search the internet to answer general questions that go beyond your report data. This is especially useful for benchmarking, industry context, or any question that requires up-to-date external information. 

Joule cites its sources inline within the response text. At the bottom of each web search response, a Sources chip displays the total number of citations - choose it to open the Sources panel, where each citation is listed as a clickable link that opens the source in a new browser tab. 

Example questions that trigger web search: 

  • "What is the current population of Japan, and how has it changed?" 
  • "What are the latest SFDR regulations in Europe?" 

Inline Report Editor 

Joule is not just for answering questions - it can also help you improve the textual content of your report. The inline report editor becomes available when you are in edit mode on a report. 

How to use it: 

  • Switch the report to edit mode. 
  • Select any text paragraph (or multiple paragraphs) in the report. 
  • The Joule inline report editor appears.
  • Enter your editing prompt: 
    • "Rewrite this in a more formal tone." 
    • "Shorten this paragraph." 
    • "Translate this to German." 
  • Joule rewrites the selected text based on your prompt. 
  • To stop generation while Joule is writing, click the Stop button in the inline editor window. 
  • To revert the change, use Undo in the report header toolbar. Redo is also available to reapply it. 

Note: 

  • The inline report editor works solely with the selected text - it is not context-aware of the surrounding report content. Non-textual elements (images, charts) within multi-paragraph selections are preserved. 
  • When selecting multiple paragraphs with different fonts, the rewritten text adopts the font of the first selected paragraph. 

Joule Without SAP AI Core Service 

Joule is available in Report Management even if your tenant has not configured an SAP AI Core destination. A built-in Joule capability is included with the SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model subscription - no SAP BTP setup required. 

If you open Administration → Settings and the Joule AI Settings section shows empty dropdowns with no destination selected (as shown below), your users are currently using Joule in the built-in mode. 

What It Offers 
  • Access to Joule conversation features (report analysis, charts, history, rename/delete, etc.) 
  • Works out of the box - no SAP AI Core or BTP destination configuration needed 
  • Ideal for occasional questions and simple ad hoc analysis 
Usage Limits (Fair Use Policy) 

A Fair Use Policy applies, with a predefined daily usage limit per user. The system will inform you when you are approaching these limits or when they have been reached. After the daily limit is reached, Joule remains available, but responses may be slower due to automatic throttling. 

Web search is currently the only feature not available in the built-in Joule mode — all other features listed above are fully supported 

Note: 

  • If you use the default (Application) subscription with the embedded SAP HANA Cloud database, no additional configuration is required. 
  • If you use a standalone SAP HANA Cloud database (BYOD Application subscription), you need to activate Natural Language Processing (NLP) in your SAP HANA Cloud instance to enable this feature. 

For setup instructions, see Use Joule Without AI Core Service in Standard Model Reporting on the SAP Help Portal. 

For teams that use Joule frequently, require higher throughput, or need web search capabilities, integrating SAP AI Core via SAP BTP is recommended. This provides access to Joule with no daily limits and extended model options - see the integration blog post and the SAP Help Portal links in the Useful Links sections. 

 Key Takeaways 

Joule in SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model is more than a Q&A tool - it brings together report analysis, chart insights, file analysis, web search, and inline editing into a single integrated AI assistant. Whether you are analyzing complex profitability reports, uploading external files for cross-referencing, searching the web for benchmarks, or rewriting report text, Joule is there to help you work smarter and faster. 

For organizations ready for higher throughput and web search capabilities, SAP AI Core integration remains the recommended path. 

Useful Links 

  • Integration blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud SM: Enhancing Report Management with the Power of Generative AI Copilot – Joule 
  • Help Portal - Joule AI Settings: Joule AI Settings 
  • Help Portal - Joule in Reports: Using Joule in Report Management 
  • Help Portal - SAP Business AI (Joule) Setup: SAP Business AI (Joule) Setup 

Thank you for reading!  Stay tuned, we'll be sharing more on Joule AI across other applications - keep an eye out!

entry.title: Joule Studio at SAP Sapphire 2026: Ten Days, Two Cities, One Clear Signal
entry.content:
As a Product Manager for SAP Build, focused on Early Adopter and Product Enablement, SAP Sapphire is one of the most important moments in the year - not just to present what we have built, but to hear directly from the people building with it. This year, the SAP Build PM team was deeply embedded across both events: in the ASUG pre-conference, the keynotes, the sessions, and the Agent Lab. We asked our PM colleagues to do what product managers do best - observe closely, listen carefully, and capture what they saw. This post is the result of that: a collective picture of what Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire looked and felt like, from the people who were there.

SAP Sapphire 2026 was held across two cities - Orlando (May 11–13) and Madrid (May 19–21) - and the energy in both was unmistakable. Joule Studio made its public debut at Sapphire. Thousands of customers, partners, and developers experienced it hands-on: some for the first time, some building on what they had already seen in Florida. The conversation that started in the US continued in Europe with the same momentum and the same conclusion. Enterprise AI has stopped being a promise and started being something you can build in an afternoon.

 

Orlando: Learning by Building

SAP Sapphire Orlando set the tone for what this year's event would be about. Christian Klein opened the keynote with a question that reframes everything: will SAP even be a software company in the future? His answer - that SAP is becoming a business AI company - was not defensive. It was clarifying.

The core argument landed clearly: AI keeps stalling in enterprises not because the models are lacking, but because they are trained on the public internet, not on your business. They have no knowledge of your processes, your data, or your compliance framework. SAP Business AI Platform - and Joule Studio within it - is built around fixing exactly that.

Christian Klein introducing SAP Business AI Platform during the Keynote

Joule Studio was introduced as an intent-based, deeply contextualized build environment for agents, open to any third-party model with no lock-in and both low-code and pro-code paths - with agent development free and backed by a €100M+ ecosystem investment. Throughout the event, there was a noticeable shift away from isolated AI use cases toward AI-infused end-to-end experiences. Attendees were not looking for another AI experiment - they wanted proof that this works in production. Real-world customer journeys, with Fujitsu cited as a concrete reference multiple times, drove that conversation.

Muhammad Alam introducing SAP Autonomous Suite during the Keynote

The keynote by Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Maria Kondratyeva featured a live Joule Studio demonstration that became the defining moment of the event. The team demonstrated a 3-way invoice matching agent built end-to-end on stage. The scenario was immediately relatable: a company processing 50,000 invoices monthly, chronic matching exceptions, and approvals consistently landing with the wrong people.

Joule Studio received a natural language description of the problem. What followed was automated and complete: SAP LeanIX integration discovering the relevant SAP systems, SAP Signavio applying 50 years of SAP process expertise to understand matching strategies and routing rules, generated orchestration workflows for exception handling and escalation, and Agent Hub providing governance from the first deployment.

The result: Accounts Payable (AP) exception cycle time reduced from 72 hours to 90 seconds. Five manual touchpoints eliminated to zero.

One audience reaction said it all: “I couldn't tell if it was a real system or a guided demo.”  That comment - part skepticism, part disbelief - repeated throughout the week. The bar for proving production-readiness is high. Joule Studio cleared it.

Christian Klein introducing the Autonomous Enterprise during the Keynote

 

The Agent Lab: From Skepticism to Conviction

If the keynote introduced Joule Studio, the Agent Lab made it personal. Over three days, more than 1,000 unique customers and partners queued up and nobody left early. The numbers are striking: 3,366 AI solutions created, 1,640 agents built, across Finance, HR, Supply Chain, and beyond.

The Agent Lab in Orlando

The experience followed a consistent pattern: skepticism at the start, hands-on building in the middle, and genuine surprise at the end. The barrier was never ideas - it was trust that the platform would actually work. Once they saw their own business problem translated into a working design, that trust arrived instantly.

As Samir Hamichi from the Agent Lab team observed: “People were looking to know the added value from SAP, what [was] the differentiator. When they walked through the experience, they understood the power of tools and strategy.”

The Agent Lab in Orlando

 

What Makes Joule Studio Different

Every AI coding tool on the market can write code. What they cannot do is understand your business. Generic tools operate at the file and repository level - they have no knowledge of your procure-to-pay process, your enterprise architecture, or the SAP capabilities already available to you. Context is lost at every handoff.

Joule Studio changes the starting point entirely. The SAP Knowledge Graph, process models from SAP Signavio, enterprise architecture from SAP LeanIX, and SAP Domain Models all flow automatically into every development artifact from the first session. A business analyst describes a problem in plain language. Joule Studio generates a structured, SAP-context-aware Product Requirements Document - a shared source of truth for business stakeholders, developers, and architects, aligned from the first output rather than after weeks of back-and-forth. That PRD becomes a Technical Specification; the specification becomes production-grade, clean-core-aligned code, deployable to a fully managed runtime.

The insight heard repeatedly across the event from customers: “This brings business back into the loop.” And from one partner architect: “Context at scale - Signavio, LeanIX. That is powerful.”

One participant shared on social media what struck with him most: the end-to-end completeness. From describing the intent to generating a PRD, functional and technical specifications, unit tests, and a deployable solution - all without leaving the platform. What surprised him most was that the platform itself guides you through the process, asking clarifying questions like a colleague would rather than expecting you to have all the answers upfront. As he put it, you are not just building agents - you are building them with the help of agents.

You are not building an agent that connects to your business afterward. You are building one that is grounded in it from the first sentence you type.

SAP Business AI Platform

 

The Governance Conversation Nobody Skips

One exchange at the Orlando Agent Lab deserves to be named directly. An attendee, after seeing what agents could do, said: “Now I can cut half my company.”

Maria Kondratyeva pushed back immediately: “That is not what agents are for. Agents eliminate soul-crushing manual work - data entry, chasing approvals, reconciling exceptions - so people can focus on work that requires judgment, creativity, and relationship-building. The work only humans can do.”

But the fact that the question was asked reflects how seriously people are taking this. The governance features in Joule Studio - Agent Hub, LeanIX integration, and human-in-the-loop approval gates - are not compliance checkboxes. They are what separates enterprise AI from unmanageable risk. The governance story needs to be as prominent as the capability story - and across both cities, the sessions that included it landed better every time.

 

Madrid: The Momentum Crosses the Atlantic

One week later, Madrid picked up exactly where Orlando left off. Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Soufia Maherzi brought the same invoice matching demonstration to a European audience and got the same reaction. A fresh crowd, the same disbelief turning into belief.

The Madrid Agent Lab wrapped up with 765 customers and partners, 1,350 AI solutions created, and 770 agents built. Different city, same energy: long queues, nobody leaving, and that consistent moment of surprise when an attendee's business problem became a fully designed agent before their eyes.

As Friederike Marby recalled: “Agent Lab was intense in the best possible way. The sheer volume of feedback, the questions, the ideas flying around. We had customers who flat-out didn't believe it was real, almost looking for reasons to dismiss it. But what really stayed with me was watching them discover, on their own terms, that it absolutely was.”

 The Madrid Agent Lab Team

 

Final Thoughts

What struck us most, putting this post together, was the consistency of what our colleagues reported across both cities - independent observations that told the same story. The skepticism at the keynote. The energy in the Agent Lab. The moment attendees stopped asking “can this really work?” and started asking “what do I build first?”

That shift - from doubt to conviction, not through a polished pitch but through direct, hands-on experience - is exactly what we set out to enable. Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire was not just a product launch. It was the beginning of a much larger conversation between SAP and the enterprise community about what it means to build with AI in a way that is grounded, governed, and genuinely useful.

We are grateful to our PM colleagues whose impressions and observations brought this post to life - and to every customer, partner, and developer who showed up, asked hard questions, and built something.

 

What is Next

Across two cities and six days of hands-on building, the combined numbers tell their own story: thousands of agents created, thousands of attendees who arrived skeptical and left with a deployment plan.

Joule Studio is planned to reach General Availability in Q3 2026. The trial is free, and the starting point is a business problem you already know you have.

 

Want to watch the sessions?

- Listen to the keynote - The Beginning of Better

- Discover the Demo - The Business AI Platform for your future

- Watch the Deep Dive - From intent to execution: Build AI agents with Joule Studio

- Hear the Strategy - The enterprise path to agentic AI

All Sapphire recordings are available here

Register for upcoming webinars here

 

Ready to start building?

Visit Joule Studio to learn more and register your interest.

Explore the Autonomous Enterprise Learning Journey for guided tutorials and deeper dives into specific capabilities.

The enterprise AI era is not approaching. In Orlando and Madrid, SAP customers and partners are already building it, one agent at a time.

entry.title: From Agent to MCP: What Building an MCP Taught Me About the Future of AI Integration
entry.content:
1. The moment that sparked the question

I've been building automations for a few years now. Not because someone asked me to — because I genuinely wanted to see if it worked.

It started with RPA. And honestly, for what it was, it worked. It solved real problems. But every time a process changed, the bot broke. Every time something shifted upstream, I was back in the designer fixing selectors and re-mapping fields. It was useful — just fragile in a way that bothered me.

Then I added LLMs to the mix. Suddenly the automation could handle variability, could read a document instead of just clicking through a form. That was exciting. But now I had two systems with completely different failure modes sitting next to each other, and I was the glue.

So I went further. I built a custom agent from scratch — a proper one, with skills, context management, decision logic. I wrote about it here. That project taught me more about how AI systems actually behave than anything I'd read. And when it worked, it really worked.

But there's always a next thing to build.

When Joule Work Desktop was announced at Sapphire, MCP was the integration standard. I didn't overthink it — if I wanted to connect to Joule, that was the path. What I didn't expect was how much building an MCP would make me question everything I thought I already understood.

The surface looks familiar: you're still giving a model tools to call, still thinking about context, still asking yourself what the model needs to know to be useful. But the architecture underneath is different in ways that matter. And those differences aren't obvious until you're in the middle of building.

This post is about what I found there.

2. Two ways to give an LLM context: Agents with Skills vs. MCP

Both approaches solve the same fundamental problem: the model doesn't know anything about your system unless you tell it.

You define what tools exist. You describe what they do. The model decides when and how to use them. That mechanic is identical in both. If you've built one, the mental model for the other isn't foreign. You're not starting from zero.

That's where the similarity ends — and where it gets interesting.

What surprised me about the agent

I'll be honest: I went into the agent build fully expecting to suffer.

Years of writing integrations the hard way had trained me well. I had my mental checklist ready: parse the API response, map the fields, pass the values explicitly between steps, handle the edge cases nobody documented. I was prepared for a week of plumbing before I could write a single line of actual logic.

Then I typed "for each result, do this" — and the agent just... did it. A proper for-each. No code. No explicit loop. I called an API and the response landed in context already mapped, already interpretable, ready to use in the next step. The model looked at the shape of the data and figured it out.

I sat there for a second genuinely unsure if it had worked or if I was about to discover a very elegant failure.

It had worked.

That moment reset a lot of assumptions I didn't know I was carrying. The plumbing I had prepared to build never needed to exist. What was left was only the part that actually required my brain: the business rules, the scoring logic, the decision criteria. The stuff that actually matters.

What's different about MCP

With the custom agent, I was still the director. The agent had skills, the skills called APIs, I defined the flow, I could see everything happening. It was my system behaving the way I told it to.

MCP is a different mental model — and it took me a moment to fully land on it.

You build a server. You expose tools. And then you let go.

The client — in this case Joule Desktop — connects to your server, discovers what you've built, and decides how to use it based on what the user asks. You don't orchestrate the flow. You don't manage the context. You define the capability and the client figures out the rest.

The first time I really internalized that, my instinct was mild panic. What do you mean I don't control the flow? But then something clicked: I wasn't building an agent anymore. I was building an interface. A set of capabilities that any MCP-compatible client could plug into — today Joule, tomorrow something else entirely. That's not losing control. That's actually a better kind of architecture.

The real difference: who's in charge

  Custom Agent (Skills) MCP

Who orchestratesThe agentThe client (Joule, Claude, etc.)
Where context livesInside the agentInside the client
Who can use the toolsOnly that agentAny MCP-compatible client
DeploymentPlatform-boundIndependent server
What you controlEverythingThe tools and their outputs

Neither is better. The agent gave me more control and visibility — which was exactly right for a workflow that needed precise, predictable steps. MCP gave me portability and decoupling — which was exactly right for something I wanted any compatible client to use without rebuilding from scratch.

The question isn't which one is superior. The question is what you're actually trying to build.

 

3. What surprised me while building

The design changed three times. And that was the real lesson.

When I start a new server, my brain goes straight to the same place it always does: define the endpoints, figure out the data model, write the code. I've done it enough times that it's almost automatic.

That instinct completely failed me here.

The first version had too many tools, each doing too little. Classic API thinking — small, focused, composable. The kind of thing that looks great in a diagram and makes you feel like a responsible adult. The model looked at my beautiful diagram and chose the wrong tool anyway. Every time. It was combining things I hadn't anticipated, skipping steps that seemed obvious, and generally behaving like it had never seen my very clean diagram. Because it hadn't. Because it doesn't work that way.

I went back in. Second version: fewer tools, each doing more. The model seemed happier. I was not. I now had tools that behaved differently depending on the input, and I couldn't clearly explain what they did even to myself. That's usually a sign something has gone wrong.

Third version — something finally clicked. And what changed wasn't the code. It was how I was thinking about the whole thing.

You're not designing endpoints for a developer to call. You're designing capabilities for a model to reason about. The name you give a tool shapes when the model reaches for it. The description isn't documentation — it's an instruction. You're writing for a different kind of reader, and once that landed for me, the design stopped fighting back.

A rule I wish someone had told me on day one: don't start building a tool until you can describe it in one sentence to a non-developer. If you can't explain it clearly, the model won't figure it out either. Write the descriptions before you write the code. Yes, before.

Then I ran into the actual hard part. And it wasn't the server.

The server was running. The tools were working. I was feeling pretty good about myself.

And then someone asked a real question and the answer came back technically correct, well-structured, and completely useless.

That's the failure mode nobody puts in the tutorial. A model without the right context doesn't throw an error — it generates a confident, beautifully formatted response that says absolutely nothing of value. It sounds like it knows what it's talking about. It doesn't. And that's scarier than a stack trace because at least a stack trace tells you something is broken.

I ended up spending more time going back to fix the knowledge layer than I'd spent building the server in the first place. Cleaning structure, rethinking what actually needed to be there versus what was just noise dressed up as content. All work that would have been faster — and less humbling — if I'd treated it as the first problem to solve instead of the thing I'd get to eventually.

The lesson, painfully earned: LLMs don't create knowledge. They surface it. Put noise in, get noise back — in a very professional tone. The knowledge layer is the foundation. The server is just the delivery mechanism. I had those two things backwards for longer than I'd like to admit.

The part I didn't expect to enjoy: vector search

I'll be honest — before this project, "embeddings" was one of those words I nodded at confidently without fully understanding.

The vector search itself went smoother than expected. Once it was running it felt almost like cheating — you send a plain question in natural language and the right content comes back ranked by actual meaning, not keyword overlap. No brittle query strings. No AND/OR logic. Just... the right stuff, most of the time.

But getting there sent me down a rabbit hole I had not budgeted for. What embeddings actually are. Why the model you use to generate them changes what comes back. Why chunking strategy — how you split and index the source content — matters way more than it sounds like it should. Why two distance metrics that seem equivalent can return completely different results.

I didn't plan for any of that. I came out of it genuinely glad I went in — and slightly annoyed that nobody had handed me a map.

4. MCP on SAP BTP: the trust boundary as a feature

There's a version of this that lives somewhere else. A different cloud provider, external APIs for embeddings, a third-party vector database. It would have worked technically.

I didn't build that version.

The decision to run the MCP on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry wasn't just about where to host something. It was about where the knowledge base lives — and what that means when you're building inside an enterprise ecosystem.

The institutional knowledge powering the server comes from SAP sources. Running on BTP meant that authentication, connectivity, and data access were all handled through mechanisms that already existed and were already trusted. No external service credentials to manage. No custom connectors to build. No explaining to anyone why sensitive knowledge was touching a third-party API.

That last one matters more than it sounds.

In enterprise settings, the conversation about data governance can easily become longer than the conversation about the actual product. Running inside the platform's trust boundary meant that conversation was mostly resolved before it started. The data doesn't leave the ecosystem. Embeddings are generated through services already inside it. The vector search runs natively. The MCP sits on Cloud Foundry. It's the same platform all the way through — and in this context, that's a feature, not a constraint.

Using the same platform as both a data store and a vector engine was the piece that made the architecture feel coherent. I wasn't stitching together the best individual tool for each layer and hoping they'd talk to each other cleanly. One platform, one trust model, one less thing to explain to anyone who asked.

The boundary wasn't something I worked around. It was something I designed with.

5. Under the hood

I want to keep this section honest and specific. Not a sales pitch for any particular stack — just the actual pieces, how they connect, and the decisions that shaped them. 

Design

The server itself

The MCP is built in Python using FastMCP — the high-level wrapper from the official MCP SDK. If you've never used it, the experience is surprisingly close to writing a FastAPI app: you define functions, decorate them with @McP.tool(), write a proper docstring, and that's your tool. The docstring isn't optional ceremony — it's what the model reads to decide when and how to call the function. Treat it like an API contract written for a reader who can't ask clarifying questions.

@mcp.tool()
def retrieve_similar_cases(query: str, category: str, top_k: int = 3) -> str:
    """
    Retrieve similar historical cases from the vector store.
    Returns expert-validated signals: category, effort, timeline, impact.
    Use these as directional hints — NOT as the final answer.
    """
    ...

The vector search

This is where the retrieval happens. The core is a HANA Cloud query using COSINE_SIMILARITY against a REAL_VECTOR column — filtered by a dimension, ordered by semantic proximity:

SELECT TOP 3 *
FROM KNOWLEDGE_TABLE
WHERE CATEGORY = ?
ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC

Before this query runs, the incoming text gets converted into an embedding vector via SAP AI Core. That vector is what gets compared against the stored embeddings in HANA. The model that generates the embeddings matters — consistency between ingestion time and query time is non-negotiable. If you embed your knowledge base with model A and query with model B, the cosine similarities will be meaningless. Same model, both directions, always.

For batch processing, the searches run in parallel using ThreadPoolExecutor — one connection per thread, all results collected before returning. The difference in latency between sequential and parallel retrieval across a large batch is significant enough that this wasn't optional.

Two vector indexes, one query pattern

The knowledge base has two separate tables, each with its own EMBEDDING column and its own filter logic. Both are queried using the same cosine similarity pattern, just with different filters and column mappings. The retrieval function runs both searches concurrently and returns a unified result keyed by source type:

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(source_types)) as pool:
    results = dict(pool.map(_search_one, source_types))

Simple, but effective. The model gets both result sets in a single tool call and uses them independently when synthesizing the response.

The transport layer on BTP

On Cloud Foundry, the server runs as a standard Python app bound to a port via the $PORT environment variable. No custom buildpack, no containers — just a Procfile, a manifest.yml, and the app itself. The MCP endpoint is exposed over HTTPS at the app's route, which is what Joule Desktop connects to. Authentication and routing are handled by BTP's built-in infrastructure.

The only thing worth calling out: stateless_http=True in the FastMCP init. MCP sessions over HTTP are stateless by design here — each request carries its own context. That's the right model for a server that multiple clients might connect to simultaneously, and it's what made the BTP deployment straightforward without needing session affinity or sticky routing.

6. Where MCP fits in the SAP AI ecosystem

I want to be careful here not to oversell something I'm still learning. But I also think there's something real happening and it's worth saying out loud.

MCP is gaining momentum inside the SAP ecosystem faster than most integration standards do. And I think the reason is simpler than it seems: it solves a problem that everyone building with AI on SAP has been quietly struggling with — how do you give a model structured access to enterprise capabilities without building a custom integration every single time?

Skills were a good answer. They worked. But they were tightly coupled to a specific agent, on a specific platform, for a specific use case. Every new scenario meant starting over.

MCP decouples that. You build the capability once. Any compatible client can use it. Today that's Joule Desktop. Tomorrow it'll be whatever comes after Joule Desktop, and the MCP you built won't care. That's the kind of architectural bet that tends to age well.

Joule Work Desktop is already in pilot. MCP is the integration path they chose. That's not a coincidence — that's a direction. And the developers who understand how to build MCPs well right now, before it's mainstream, before there are established patterns and ready-made templates for everything, are going to have a significant head start on everyone who waits until it's the obvious thing to do.

I'm not saying drop everything and rewrite your agents as MCPs. I'm saying: if you've been curious, now is a genuinely good time to build something small and see what you learn. The protocol is approachable. The tooling is improving fast. And the mental shift it forces — from thinking about functions to thinking about capabilities a model can reason about — is one of those shifts that changes how you see the whole problem space, not just this one project.

Build something. Even if it's small. Especially if it's small.

The best way to understand what MCP actually changes is to be in the middle of building with it and suddenly realize your old instincts aren't working anymore. That moment of friction is where the learning is. Don't skip it by reading about it — go find it.

entry.title: End to End Guide: Enabling SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad (PCE + OnPremise PoC)
entry.content:
1. Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI, designed to help users accomplish tasks through natural language interaction within SAP applications. This guide will provide a step by step walkthrough to enable joule in SAP Fiori.

The guide is primarily written for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition (PCE).
PS: Also covers steps that differ for On-Premise S/4HANA (for a Proof-of-Concept environment).

2. Component Overview

Before starting, it is important to understand the key components involved and the role each plays.

Component

What It Is

Role in This Integration

SAP Joule

SAP's generative AI copilot. A cloud service on SAP BTP.

The AI assistant end users interact with. Loaded in FLP header as a Shell Plugin.

SAP BTP

SAP Business Technology Platform  the cloud foundation.

Hosts Joule, Work Zone, Identity Services, and destinations connecting to S/4HANA.

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

SAP's digital workplace platform. Can serve as launchpad or content bridge.

Acts as the technical bridge between Joule and S/4HANA. Required in the Joule Formation.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

SAP's Identity Authentication Service. Manages user identity and SSO.

Provides the SSO layer. All BTP services require IAS authentication.

SAP Cloud Connector (SCC)

An on-premise agent creating a secure tunnel from BTP to on-premise systems.

Enables Work Zone and Joule on BTP to securely reach S/4HANA.

SAP Fiori Launchpad (FLP)

The S/4HANA web UI hub where users launch Fiori apps.

The surface where the Joule icon appears via a Shell Plugin target mapping.

Formation

A BTP System Landscape construct grouping systems for a specific integration.

Tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together.

CDM3

Common Data Model v3 . SAP Fiori's content model (catalogs, tiles, groups).

Used to expose S/4HANA Fiori content to BTP so Work Zone can sync it.

3. Architecture Overview

The following describes the end-to-end integration flow:

  • End User opens S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad in browser
  • FLP authenticates via IAS (after SSO is configured)
  • FLP loads the Joule Shell Plugin from the Joule Web Client (sapdas.cloud.sap)
  • Joule on BTP connects to Work Zone via the Joule Formation
  • Work Zone reaches S/4HANA via BTP Destinations → Cloud Connector → ABAP Server(S/4 system)
  • Joule icon appears in FLP header. Users interact with Joule through natural language.

4. Prerequisites

Requirement

Details

SAP BTP Global Account

Admin access. Subaccount in the same region as your IAS tenant.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

IAS tenant associated with your BTP Global Account.

SAP S/4HANA System

PCE or On-Premise (for PoC). FLP accessible via browser.

SAP Cloud Connector

Installed and running. Registered to your BTP subaccount.

S/4HANA Technical User

User with access to CDM3 service (/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3) and FLP admin roles.

BTP Entitlements

Joule (foundation), SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Regional Alignment

BTP subaccount, IAS tenant, and Work Zone must be in the same region.

Note: PCE vs On-Premise: PCE uses token-based registration via Communication Arrangements. On-Premise uses Cloud Connector and manual destinations. Each step calls out differences.

5. Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Enable Cloud Foundry in BTP Subaccount

Why: Cloud Foundry is the runtime where BTP service instances (Work Zone, Joule) are deployed. Without it, subscriptions and the Joule booster cannot run.

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit → navigate to your target Subaccount → Overview.
  2. Click Enable Cloud Foundry. Accept defaults (Plan: standard). Click Create.
  3. Once enabled, click Create Space → name it e.g. 'dev'. Assign yourself Space Developer + Space Manager roles.

Validation: Subaccount Overview shows CF Enabled, Org Name, API Endpoint, and space 'dev' listed.

On-Premise PoC: No difference. This step is identical for both PCE and On-Premise.

 

Step 2: Add Entitlements

Why: Entitlements grant your subaccount the right to use specific BTP services. Without them, the services cannot be subscribed to or instantiated.

  1. Go to Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments.
  2. Select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.
  3. Add: Joule (also search as 'das-application') — plan: foundation
  4. Add: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard
  5. Add: SAP Cloud Identity Services — plan: connectivity
  6. Click Save.

Validation: All three services appear in Entitlements list with quota assigned.

Step 3: Establish Trust to IAS

Why: All BTP services (including Joule) authenticate via IAS. Establishing trust allows your subaccount to delegate authentication to IAS, enabling SSO across all connected services.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration.
  2. Click Establish Trust.
  3. Select your IAS tenant (e.g., yourtenantid.accounts.ondemand.com).
  4. Click Next through the wizard → Finish.

Validation: Trust Configuration shows your IAS tenant with status Active.

Step 4: Configure Trusted Domains in BTP

Why: BTP Trusted Domains control which browser origins can interact with your subaccount's services via iFrames and cross-origin calls. Without this, the Joule panel cannot be embedded in your FLP.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Settings → Trusted Domains.
  2. Click Add for each of the following origins (scheme + host + port if non-standard):
  • Your FLP origin: e.g. https://your-s4-host:44301
